source.title: Add hook leader-elector, checksum-based idempotency and migration dry-run values for private installer
source.author: Diego Morales
source.description: Motivation: on air-gapped and multi-operator installs we observed multiple init/postupgrade hooks racing which caused duplicate migrations and transient secret reconciliation failures. This PR introduces a lightweight leader-elector pattern for Helm hook jobs, a checksum-based idempotency guard to short-circuit unchanged runs, and a configurable migration dry-run mode intended for safer private upgrades. Summary of changes: 1) templates/_hook_helpers.tpl - new leaderElector macro and checksum helpers; 2) templates/checksum-configmap.yaml - create a stable configmap with a computed checksum footprint for user-supplied migration/config; 3) templates/init-job.yaml and templates/migration-job.yaml - use leader election annotation and skip when checksum matches; 4) values.schema.json and values.yaml - add migrate.dryRun, hooks.leaderElector.enabled, hooks.leaderElector.lockTTL, hooks.checksum.enabled and externalLoadBalancer.annotations passthrough; 5) tests/integration/migration-dryrun.sh - CI integration test that validates dry-run no-op behavior. Design notes: leader-elector is implemented as a conservative ConfigMap-based lock annotation (no extra controller) so it works in air-gapped clusters without additional CRDs; lock TTL is configurable and defaults to 10m to avoid long stuck locks in failed runs. Checksum logic ties to a canonical set of keys (config, secrets, job templates) so trivial template re-renders do not re-trigger heavy migrations. The migrate.dryRun flag runs schema validation and a noop path for database migrations and reports a JSON summary to stdout for operator review. Testing and rollout: unit and chart-lint added; integration migration-dryrun test runs in CI; deployment plan is to enable the feature behind values defaults (leaderElector.enabled = true, migrate.dryRun = false) and rollout to internal canary clusters first. Backwards compatibility: all hooks remain functional if feature flags are disabled. See linked Linear tickets for rollout and post-merge follow-ups.
commits: a1c8f2c: add _hook_helpers.tpl with leaderElector and checksum helpers
b2d9e4a: introduce checksum-configmap template and wiring into init/migration jobs
c3f1a67: add migrate.dryRun support and flow guards to migration-job.yaml
d4e2b98: update values.schema.json and example values, add externalLoadBalancer.annotation passthrough
e5f7c03: add integration test for migration dry-run and update README usage notes
review_comments: Ava Chen: Overall looks good — appreciate the conservative leader-elector approach. Please add a small unit template test to validate the ConfigMap lock keys and TTL logic.
Diego Morales: Added unit snapshot in tests/templates/leader-elector.test.yaml and a short explanation in the helmsman test harness. PTAL.
Lina Kaur: Can we make the default lockTTL shorter (e.g. 5m) for faster recovery in CI? Also request explicit ownerReferences on the checksum ConfigMap to avoid orphaned objects during uninstall.
Diego Morales: Lowered default to 10m after discussion (tradeoff for long-running migrations). Added ownerReferences when running under helm controller mode; documented behavior in README.

Marcus Lee: Integration test failure on first CI run showed a race condition — job pod started before lock ConfigMap was observed. Suggest adding a short readiness wait loop in the job entrypoint when leaderElector.enabled=true.
Diego Morales: Added a 3s backoff loop and retried CI. All checks green now. Marking as ready to merge.
Ava Chen: Approved. Verified manual canary on internal cluster and migration dry-run produced expected no-op logs.
source.release_notes: Private: add conservative leader-elector for Helm hook jobs to avoid duplicate migrations; introduce checksum-based idempotency to skip unchanged hook runs; add migrate.dryRun for safe upgrade validation; new values.schema.json additions and templating helpers.

source.title: helm: introduce cost-profiles chart with QuantConfig CRD and exporter
source.author: Marcus Li
source.description: Motivation: Provide a Helm-native way to ship operator-friendly defaults for batching, cache presets, and quantization profiles so platform deployers can opt into cost-optimization automation during chart install/upgrade. This PR adds a new 'cost-profiles' Helm chart that bundles a QuantConfig CRD, a lightweight exporter sidecar that emits curated presets to the control-plane API, and opinionated values for batching/caching defaults. Key features: 1) QuantConfig CRD: a cluster-scoped CRD model that encodes quantization tiers (fp16, int8, q4_0), acceptable accuracy degradation thresholds, and rollout tags; 2) exporter: a small Deployment (exporter sidecar) that reads installed CR instances and posts signed preset bundles to the Redwood control plane or dumps them to a configured S3 bucket; 3) values.yaml presets: safe-by-default batch sizes, KV cache TTLs, and host-level annotations to opt deployments into conservative or aggressive profiles; 4) upgrade path: chart registers/validates existing QuantConfig objects and runs a dry-run export during helm upgrade by default. Backwards compatibility: existing clusters that don't install this chart will be unaffected; the CRD is optional and installs as an opt-in. Security: exporter uses ephemeral service account with RBAC limited to reading the CRD and writing to a configured outbound endpoint; TLS and token-auth are enforced. Testing: unit templates tested via helm unittest; end-to-end smoke tests run in the staging cluster to ensure installer doesn't change existing deployments; we exercised the exporter against a local mock control-plane and an S3 sink; load tests verified default batch sizes do not increase median latency beyond the 95th-percent SLO for small models. Checklist: - [x] Add Chart scaffolding + README - [x] Implement QuantConfig CRD template and validation schema - [x] Add exporter manifest + ConfigMap-driven templates - [x] Values presets + examples for conservative/aggressive modes - [x] Unit tests (helm-unittest) - [x] E2E smoke in staging - [x] Security review and RBAC policy - [x] Docs entry and upgrade notes How to test locally: helm install ./cost-profiles --set exporter.sink=mock --namespace redwood-test; verify crd present with kubectl get quantconfigs.redwood.ai; create a QuantConfig and watch exporter logs. Release notes: Adds cost-profiles Helm chart that enables cluster operators to export curated batching/caching defaults and quantization presets to the Redwood control plane; opt-in CRD and exporter; no automatic application of presets to runtime without explicit operator approval. Migration notes: None required for existing installs; operators who adopt should review preset thresholds in values.yaml. Related: ENG-3421 (helm presets design), ENG-4277 (quant profile schema).
commits: Initial chart scaffolding and README (Chart.yaml + README.md)
Add QuantConfig CRD template and openapi schema
Implement exporter Deployment and ConfigMap templating
Add values presets for conservative/aggressive modes and examples
Unit tests for templates and basic policy validation
Fix RBAC scope for exporter service account; add TLS enforcement in exporter config
Add e2e smoke test script for staging and helm test harness
review_comments: Ava Chen: Nice enclosure of the CRD and the exporter. Please reduce the default 'aggressive' batch size for seq_len>512 as we've seen SLO regressions in perf-canary; also add a guard in the exporter to avoid repeated POSTs on transient network flaps.
Marcus Li: Thanks Ava — lowered aggressive default for seq_len>512 from 64 to 32 and added exponential backoff to the exporter retry loop (configurable in values.yaml).
Diego Morales: RBAC LGTM but can we scope the Role to the chart namespace instead of cluster-wide? If the exporter only needs to read the CRD in the same namespace, that'd be better for security.
Marcus Li: Updated role to namespace scope and adjusted exporter to watch namespace-local CR instances; documented the change in README and upgrade notes.
Priya Nair: Can you add a helm test that creates a QuantConfig and asserts the exporter emits a valid JSON bundle to a local file sink? That would catch templating regressions.
Marcus Li: Added helm test that runs a lightweight mock file sink in a Job and validates the bundle; included tests in CI matrix.

source.title: introduce-postupgrade-cleanup-hook-and-explicit-values-schema-for-private
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Add optional post-upgrade cleanup hook and explicit values.schema.json for Redwood Private Helm chart; refactor helpers for init/postupgrade hooks; add an integration test for upgrade scenarios.
source.description:
Motivation: Private installs occasionally leave transient cluster objects (temp PVs, breadcrumb ConfigMaps) after upgrade paths that changed storage/retention policies. Operators need a safe, opt-in post-upgrade cleanup hook that can be enabled in values and validated by an explicit values.schema.json. This PR implements an optional post-upgrade cleanup Helm hook, adds a JSON schema for values validation, and refactors common helpers so init and post-upgrade hooks share templating primitives.

What I changed (summary): commits: 1) add values.schema.json and update values.yaml defaults; 2) introduce templates/hooks/post-upgrade-cleanup.yaml implementing a Job hook guarded by .Values.postUpgrade.cleanup.enabled; 3) refactor templates/_helpers.tpl to expose hook labels and common container/env helpers shared with init-job.yaml; 4) add a minimal integration test charts/private/tests/upgrade-scenario.sh that simulates a canary upgrade and asserts the cleanup job runs only when enabled; 5) small README update documenting the new flag and migration notes.

Key behaviors and decisions: - The cleanup hook is explicitly opt-in: values.postUpgrade.cleanup.enabled is false by default. When enabled the hook runs as a Helm post-upgrade hook with policy: hook-delete-policy:hook-succeeded. The job is namespaced and uses the same serviceAccount as the init-job when present. - The job is tolerant: uses exponential backoff and a --dry-run mode controlled via values.postUpgrade.cleanup.dryRun for safer rollouts. - To avoid breaking existing installs we added values.schema.json that marks all new keys as optional and provides deprecation notes for legacy top-level keys. This allows CI (helm lint --values) + custom validation to catch misconfigurations. - We refactored helpers into templates/_helpers.tpl to keep templates DRY and reduce divergence between init and post-upgrade hook logic.

Files changed (high-level): - charts/private/values.schema.json: explicit schema for postUpgrade.cleanup, initJob, and common hook controls. - charts/private/templates/hooks/post-upgrade-cleanup.yaml: CronJob->Job style post-upgrade hook (Job) that runs the cleanup container, reads retention rules from a ConfigMap and exits gracefully when nothing to clean. - charts/private/templates/init-job.yaml: small adjustments to use the shared helpers and to expose the existing sa name via helper. - charts/private/templates/_helpers.tpl: new helpers to render hook annotations, labels, and job container command composition. - charts/private/tests/upgrade-scenario.sh: basic e2e-ish script used in CI job input to validate that enabling flag causes job creation and successful completion. - charts/private/README.md: documentation for new values, examples to enable cleanup, and troubleshooting notes.

Review notes and conversation snapshot: - Diego Alvarez: requested stronger schema coverage for the dryRun field and suggested moving the default imagePullPolicy to values.global to match other charts. I addressed both and pushed a follow-up commit that updated values.schema.json and values.yaml. - Priya Natarajan: asked for an escape hatch when the cleanup job misbehaves (she requested a configurable backoffLimit and a toggle to restrict runtime). I added values.postUpgrade.cleanup.backoffLimit and a short note in README about controlling the job lifecycle. - Samir Gupta: ran the upgrade-scenario test in his dev cluster and reported a lint failure due to a missing helper; I fixed the _helpers.tpl order and re-ran CI.

CI: The pipeline originally failed one lint step due to a templating order (fixed in 2nd commit). Full CI run after fixes: helm/lint -> pass, unit/templates -> pass, integration/upgrade-scenario -> pass (the script runs in a mocked KinD job in CI and verifies job creation and logs).

Compatibility and migration: This is non-breaking by default; the cleanup job is disabled unless explicitly enabled. values.schema.json is additive and marks legacy keys as deprecated but not removed. Operators upgrading the chart can enable cleanup post-upgrade after confirming retention rules.

Testing performed: - Local helm template runs against k8s 1.26 and 1.27 manifests. - Integration script executed in CI with KinD cluster; tested both enabled and disabled states. - Manual run in a staging cluster to validate ServiceAccount permissions and RBAC scoping.

Merge outcome: Approved by reviewers after two rounds of feedback. CI green. Squash-merged into main on 2026-02-25 with a brief merge commit.

Notes for follow-ups: - Consider exposing a metrics endpoint for the cleanup job for observability (ENG-3820). - Add a safety-mode that requires a specific annotation on the release to permit cleanup in production environments.

source.title: decouple-init-configmap-and-introduce-tenant-rotation-operator-helpers
source.author: Mateo Alvarez
source.description: Motivation: In private installs we need a small, immutable init ConfigMap consumed by several init jobs and a lightweight tenant-rotation helper that can be turned on/off independently. This PR splits the monolithic init template into a minimal configmap, introduces a tenant-rotation helper template and CRD scaffold for use by on-prem operators, and tightens the values schema for preflight probes and secret mapping. Changes include a compatibility shim so existing installs are not broken; rollout plan below. source.title: namespace-scoped init phases and per-tenant template overlay support for private installs
source.author: Ava Martinez
source.release_notes: Add namespace-scoped init phases and per-tenant template overlays to the private installer Helm chart; no runtime change for existing installs unless overlay is enabled.
source.description:
Motivation: Private installs need safer, tenant-scoped initialization sequences and a maintainable way to apply per-namespace template overrides without adding global init jobs or replicating chart variants. This PR introduces two orthogonal capabilities: 1) namespace-scoped init phases that run initPods/Jobs limited to the target namespace and sequenced using phase ordering and explicit readiness gates, and 2) a lightweight template-overlay mechanism (values.overlays) that lets operators supply per-namespace templating fragments (ConfigMap-style YAML) applied via an idempotent post-render hook.

Summary of changes: added namespace-scoped init controller helpers, augmented values schema with overlays and phase ordering, introduced a post-render overlay applier job and an associated dry-run mode, updated helper library templates to support overlay merging, and added unit tests and chart documentation.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in ENG-1942 - [x] Values schema updated with validation - [x] Backwards-compatible by default (overlays disabled) - [x] Unit tests for overlay merge logic - [x] Added e2e smoke for namespace init sequencing in chart-tests CI

Commits: - 1a2b3c4: add values.overlays schema and docs - 2b3c4d5: implement overlay post-render hook and dry-run flag - 3c4d5e6: introduce namespace phase annotation and leader-election guard for init jobs - 4d5e6f7: tests + chart-tests e2e - 5e6f7g8: docs, changelog, and examples for private install templates

Files changed (high level): - charts/private/values.schema.json: add overlays, perNamespace phases and validation rules - charts/private/templates/_init_helpers.tpl: new helpers for namespacing and phase ordering - charts/private/templates/init-phase-job.yaml: new namespace-scoped Job CRD template with leader election guard - charts/private/templates/overlay-applier-job.yaml: post-render job for applying per-namespace overlays (dry-run supported) - charts/private/templates/NOTES.txt: doc snippets for enabling overlays and troubleshooting - tests/chart-tests/overlay-sequencing.sh: simple e2e check - docs/private/overlays.md: operational guide and examples - ci/chart-tests.yaml: add smoke job for namespace init sequencing

Implementation notes: - Values schema: values.overlays is an object mapping namespace names to an array of overlay fragments (strings containing YAML). Each fragment is merged in order using a conservative merge (scalars replaced, lists replaced unless marked mergeList: true). The schema is strict to avoid surprising types. - Init sequencing: each namespace init job receives annotations: redwood.io/init-phase and redwood.io/phase-ready-probe. A lightweight controller in the private-installer helm hooks orchestrates wait-for-phase-ready semantics using Kubernetes probe semantics and leader-election (ConfigMap lock) to avoid parallel init collisions across Helm upgrades. - Overlay applier: placed as a post-render Helm hook invoked conditionally (values.overlays.enabled). The applier has a --dry-run option that validates that rendered manifests would be syntactically merged and emits a report to a ConfigMap for operator inspection. It uses a deterministic merge order to keep rollbacks simple.

Review conversation (selected): - Liam Chen (reviewer): "Can we guarantee idempotency if overlay fragments change order between upgrades? Suggest adding stable sort by fragment name/hash before merge."

  Ava Martinez (author): "Good catch — implemented stable-hash sort by fragment id and added tests to ensure deterministic merges. See commit 2b3c4d5."

  Priya Nair (reviewer): "The leader election guard relies on ConfigMap locks in cluster scope — this was a concern for air-gapped installs where RBAC is locked down. Can we scope locks to the namespace?"

  Ava Martinez: "Updated to use namespace-scoped Lease objects when the target install mode is 'namespaced' and fall back to cluster-scoped locks only when explicitly configured. Documented the RBAC change in docs/private/overlays.md."

  Ethan Park (reviewer): "Please add more observability — per-namespace init phase metrics and an alerting hook if a phase stalls."

  Ava Martinez: "Added a Prometheus metric 'redwood_private_init_phase_duration_seconds' with labels namespace and phase and a simple alert recipe. Also extended NOTES.txt with common failure modes and diagnostics."

CI summary: - chart lint: pass - unit tests: pass - chart-tests (k8s 1.26 smoke): pass (overlay dry-run + namespace sequencing) - e2e extended: rerun once due to flake, passed on rerun.

Backwards compatibility: The new values keys are opt-in (values.overlays.enabled = false by default). Existing installs are unaffected. There is a small RBAC footprint change when overlays.enabled = true (namespace Lease permissions and potential ConfigMap read for dry-run reports) — documented in the release notes and the private installer upgrade guide.

Operational guidance: - To enable per-namespace overlays set values.overlays.enabled=true and populate values.overlays.namespaces.my-tenant with an ordered list of fragments. - Use overlays.dryRun=true to validate before applying; a ConfigMap 'private-overlays-dryrun-report' will contain the merge result and any validation errors. - For large fleets, prefer a centralized overlay repo and a templating preflight step in CI to avoid oversized Helm value blobs.

Merge: Squash-and-merge after last approval. Merged by Liam Chen on 2026-02-25.

source.title: conditional-init-job-and-hook-templating-to-support-idempotent-private-upgrades
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Problem / motivation: private deployments running older charts sometimes fail upgrades when init/rollback jobs are unguarded or when cluster topology (multi-az vs single-az) requires different init behavior. This caused a recent support case (SUP-122) where the init job re-ran on every helm upgrade and blocked subsequent hooks. Goals: 1) make init/rollback jobs idempotent and conditional based on values and cluster facts, 2) provide a clear values migration path for private customers upgrading from older installers, 3) keep helm hooks robust for both air-gapped on-prem installs and VPC installs, 4) add minimal integration tests to prevent regressions. What this PR changes: - values.schema.json: introduce new top-level keys private.init.runOnce (boolean), private.init.leaderElectionConfig (string), private.upgrade.mode ("canary"|"standard"), and private.hooks.enableRollback (boolean). The schema contains descriptions and default values to be surfaced in the installer UI. - templates/init-job.yaml: converts the previous unconditional Job -> a helm template that renders the Job only when .Values.private.enabled and .Values.private.init.runOnce are true OR when clusterTopology == "multi-az" (we detect via .Capabilities but allow manual override). The Job implements a lightweight idempotency check against a ConfigMap (key: redwood-private-init-leader) so only one node performs expensive migrations; the Job re-checks to avoid duplicate work on retries. - templates/hooks/preflight-crd-check.yaml: pre-install hook that verifies required CRDs exist (CRDs vary for on-prem). When CRDs are missing the hook fails fast with actionable message. - templates/hooks/rollback-job.yaml: rollback job is now gated by .Values.private.hooks.enableRollback and annotated with helm.sh/hook-weight to run before any other teardown hooks. It attempts a safe rollback probe and records results into a Kubernetes JobResult ConfigMap for operator inspection. - templates/_helpers.tpl: add sprig helpers and template snippets to centralize conditional logic (clusterTopologyOrOverride, shouldRenderInitJob). - values.yaml: sensible defaults that preserve current behavior for existing private installs (backwards-compatible defaults: runOnce: false, enableRollback: true). - docs/UPGRADE_PRIVATE.md: migration steps describing how to opt-in to runOnce, how leaderElectionConfig works, and how to set clusterTopology when Helm cannot auto-detect. - tests/upgrade-integration.sh: small CI smoke test run in kind to validate that repeated helm upgrade with runOnce=true does not re-run init work and that rollback hook executes when enabled. Notes on testing and CI: added a new pipeline job (helm-private-upgrade-smoke) to the chart CI that exercises the new template conditionals in a minimal kind cluster; this job is non-blocking but runs on merge to main. Backwards compatibility: default values keep previous behavior unless operators explicitly opt-in to runOnce or change topology. Rollout plan: merge to main, ship as patch release with clear release notes and migration guide. Checklist: - [x] schema and values documented - [x] idempotent init job implemented - [x] rollback gate and weight added - [x] tests and CI smoke job added - [x] reviewers addressed comments
commits: a1b2c3f: add values.schema.json additions for private.init and private.hooks
d4e5f6a: implement conditional init job template with leader-election configmap check
7f8e9d0: add preflight CRD check hook and rollback-job gating
1122334: helper templates (clusterTopologyOrOverride, shouldRenderInitJob) and sprig usage
4455667: docs/UPGRADE_PRIVATE.md and migration notes
8899aab: add tests/upgrade-integration.sh and CI job definition
review_comments: Omar Salazar: Flagged the leader-election ConfigMap name collision risk if multiple clusters share the same namespace; requested we namespace the ConfigMap with .Release.Name — addressed in second commit.
Priya Natarajan: Asked for clearer failure messages from preflight CRD hook so operators know remediation steps; updated hook to include CRD table and suggested kubectl commands.
Benito Alvarez: Requested we keep default behavior unchanged for existing installs — verified values.yaml keeps runOnce=false by default and added UPGRADE_PRIVATE.md migration notes.

Maya Chen: Reply to Omar and Priya explaining idempotency approach (ConfigMap-based leader lock plus job-level re-check), and noting we intentionally avoid leader-election libs to keep image small; added comment in template.
Omar Salazar: Approval after ConfigMap name change and small nit on template indentation fixed.
Priya Natarajan: Approved; suggested follow-up to add metric for init-job duration (tracked ENG-7628).

New values: private.init.runOnce, private.init.leaderElectionConfig, private.upgrade.mode, private.hooks.enableRollback. Includes migration guide and CI smoke test to prevent regressions.

source.title: helm-hook-helpers-and-values-example-generator
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Introduce a reusable helm helper for hook conditionalization; add a small generator for values.yaml examples used in docs and e2e; tighten chart-testing workflow and add a step to lint hook templates; expose a canary_init_job toggle for safer upgrades.
source.description: Context: Private deployments have accumulated ad-hoc hook conditionals across multiple charts (private-installer, private-core, private-db). This causes subtle behavior divergence during upgrades/rollbacks and bloats templates. This PR centralizes hook decision logic into a chart helper and ships a small values-example generator that emits curated values.yaml snippets used in docs and in chart-tests. Motivation: make hook behavior consistent (init/cron/pre/post hooks), simplify templating, and provide deterministic example payloads that can be baked into release notes and upgrade playbooks. Summary of changes: - Add templates/_helpers/hook_helpers.tpl: a single entrypoint to evaluate whether to render a hook and which annotations to attach based on values (canaryInit, rollbackPolicy, atomicFallback). - Replace duplicated conditional blocks in charts/private-installer/templates/init-job.yaml and charts/private-core/templates/preupgrade-cron.yaml with calls to the helper. - Add tooling/dev/generate-values-examples.py: small script that reads values.schema.json and values.yaml and generates a set of annotated example snippets for docs and chart-tests. - Add values/examples/ canary and standard examples and wire them into docs build. - Extend chart-testing workflow: run helm template with --execute for hook resources and validate that no unexpected initJobs are created when canaryInit=false. - Add chart test fixture for an upgrade path that verifies hooks run only when canary toggle and rollbackPolicy are enabled. - Add docs/helm/hooks.md with examples and a short troubleshooting section for common upgrade failures. High-level commits: commit 1: introduce hook_helpers.tpl and unit tests for template logic; commit 2: refactor init/pre/post hook templates to use helper; commit 3: add generate-values-examples.py and CI step; commit 4: add docs and chart-tests; commit 5: fix CI lint failures for template indentation and re-run chart-tests. Files changed (high level): charts/private-installer/templates/init-job.yaml; charts/private-core/templates/preupgrade-cron.yaml; charts/private-db/templates/postupgrade-hook.yaml; charts/_helpers.tpl (new); values/examples/canary.yaml; values/examples/standard.yaml; tooling/generate-values-examples.py; .github/workflows/chart-testing.yml (updated); docs/helm/hooks.md. Important implementation notes: - The helper intentionally returns a tuple-like object through named labels (renderHook, hookAnnotation) encoded as a small line-delimited string to avoid nested dicts in templating. - generate-values-examples.py is deliberately small (no external deps beyond pyyaml used in CI) so it can be run in CI and locally by engineers. - The hook helper preserves existing annotation keys used by the private installer to signal the controller to wait for job completion; behavior is unchanged unless canaryInit is toggled. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is unchanged. The canary_init_job toggle is opt-in in values.yaml and off by default. This PR is not a breaking change. Testing and CI: - Initial push failed chart-testing due to helm-lint complaining about blank lines in helper comment blocks; fixed in subsequent commit. - chart-testing now runs helm template with an added --execute arg to validate hooks render as expected. - Unit tests added for template helper using helm unittest. Review/QA notes: Please pay attention to the helper contract (inputs: .Values.hooks.canaryInit, .Values.hooks.rollbackPolicy, .Release.Revision) and edge cases where rollbackPolicy="atomic" with canaryInit=true should still fall back to a safe path. Docs: hooks.md includes migration guidance for operators upgrading from older installers and a troubleshooting checklist used by Support when customers see stuck upgrades.

source.title: charts: add failover-controller values + safe defaults (dry-run, approvals, rate limits)
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.release_notes: Adds chart support for failover-controller with conservative defaults (disabled by default, dry-run support, optional approval hooks, anti-flap/rate limits, and recommended resource/pod safety settings).
source.description:
## Summary
This PR introduces first-class Helm chart support for the regional failover automation controller. The goal is to make controlled enablement safe and consistent across staging and production, with explicit feature flags, conservative resource defaults, and guardrails that reduce risk during noisy-signal periods.

Key points:
- Adds a `failoverController` block to the platform chart values with an explicit `enabled: false` default.
- Supports dry-run mode configuration (compute and emit audit events without executing flips) as the default behavior when enabled in staging.
- Adds optional approval hooks configuration (require on-call ack prior to execution) for production safety.
- Adds rate limiting / anti-flap knobs (max actions per hour, hold-down/settle timers) exposed as values and wired into container env/config.
- Adds recommended pod safety defaults: requests/limits, PDB, topology spread constraints, and default non-root securityContext.
- Adds common observability toggles for metrics/annotations and log format (structured json).

## Why
As part of the Reliability Program’s regional failover automation upgrade, we need a reliable and repeatable way to deploy the controller across environments with minimal risk. We also want the rollout plan to be “values-driven” so we can enable per-cluster/per-tenant behavior without chart patching.

## Implementation notes
- New template `templates/failover-controller/deployment.yaml` is gated behind `.Values.failoverController.enabled`.
- `dryRun` is a values switch (`.Values.failoverController.mode: dry-run|active`) rather than a separate chart to reduce drift between staging/prod.
- Approval hooks are disabled by default and only take effect when `.Values.failoverController.approvals.enabled=true`.
- Rate limiting defaults are conservative to reduce oscillation during partial impairments:
  - `maxActionsPerHour: 2`
  - `minSecondsBetweenActions: 600`
  - `holdDownSeconds: 900`
- Resources default to low but non-trivial to avoid CPU starvation during high event volume:
  - requests: cpu 250m / memory 512Mi
  - limits: cpu 1000m / memory 1Gi
- Adds PDB with `minAvailable: 1` to avoid voluntary disruption taking down the controller.

## Values added (high level)
- `failoverController.enabled`
- `failoverController.image.repository/tag/pullPolicy`
- `failoverController.mode` (default: `dry-run` when enabled; chart default remains disabled)
- `failoverController.approvals.enabled` + webhook/timeout
- `failoverController.rateLimits.maxActionsPerHour`, `minSecondsBetweenActions`
- `failoverController.timers.holdDownSeconds`, `signalStabilitySeconds`
- `failoverController.resources.requests/limits`
- `failoverController.podDisruptionBudget.enabled/minAvailable`
- `failoverController.podAnnotations` (for metrics scraping + rollout markers)
- `failoverController.extraEnv` and `extraArgs`

## Changed areas
- charts/redwood-platform/values.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/values.schema.json
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/failover-controller/deployment.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/failover-controller/serviceaccount.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/failover-controller/pdb.yaml
- charts/redwood-platform/templates/_helpers.tpl (naming/labels helpers)
- charts/redwood-platform/README.md (document values + examples)

## Example values (staging)
Set:
- `failoverController.enabled: true`
- `failoverController.mode: dry-run`
- `failoverController.approvals.enabled: false`
- `failoverController.rateLimits.maxActionsPerHour: 10` (staging only)

## Testing
- `helm lint` on redwood-platform
- `helm template` validated for enabled/disabled paths
- CI renders manifests for both `mode=dry-run` and `mode=active` in matrix

## Rollout plan
- Land chart changes first (this PR)
- Enable in staging with `mode=dry-run`
- Promote to production with `enabled=true`, `mode=dry-run`, approvals enabled
- Once signal quality is validated via game day + burn rate stability, flip to `mode=active` per cluster with existing change approval workflow

Linked: ENG-2415 (dry-run), ENG-2416 (rollback guardrails), ENG-2411 (umbrella).
source.commits_summary: 1) charts(redwood-platform): add failoverController values block + documentation 2) templates: add failover-controller Deployment/SA/PDB gated by enabled flag 3) schema: validate enums for mode + numeric bounds for timers/rate limits 4) ops: add topologySpreadConstraints and securityContext defaults 5) ci: add helm-template matrix for enabled/disabled controller
source.review_conversation:
Miles Thornton: Can we ensure the controller is *off* by default in all environments? We shouldn’t risk accidental enablement on upgrade.
Benji Okafor (author): Yep — `failoverController.enabled` defaults to false. Also added schema so unknown keys don’t implicitly enable anything.

Rafael Mendes: For production, we want approvals on by default when `mode=active`. Can we make it hard to run active without approvals?
Benji Okafor: Added a schema rule + template guard: if `mode=active` and `approvals.enabled=false`, chart renders a warning annotation and sets `FAILOVER_REQUIRE_APPROVALS=true` unless explicitly overridden (documented). This keeps staging simple while making prod safer.

Keiko Yamamoto: Resource requests feel high for a controller; can we start lower?
Benji Okafor: Lowered CPU request from 500m to 250m, kept mem at 512Mi due to bursty evaluation + cache. Also added HPA hook placeholder but left disabled.

Allison Grant: Please add standard Prometheus scrape annotations and a stable `component=failover-controller` label for dashboards.
Benji Okafor: Done — added `podAnnotations` defaults and a required `app.kubernetes.io/component` label.

Miles Thornton: Nit: timers naming is inconsistent (`holdDownSeconds` vs `minSecondsBetweenActions`).
Benji Okafor: Kept both but grouped into `timers.*` and `rateLimits.*` for clarity; updated README examples.

Approved-by: Miles Thornton
Approved-by: Rafael Mendes
Approved-by: Allison Grant
Approved-by: Keiko Yamamoto

source.title: Expose optimization config version pinning via Helm values + document rollback procedure
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.body: Summary: This PR adds first-class Helm values to pin the active Optimization Config version for Redwood Private installs and documents an operator-friendly rollback flow (pin to prior version + apply). Motivation: In Hosted/Dedicated, the control plane manages versioning and rollback; for Private, operators need a deterministic way to (a) hold a known-good version during upgrades and (b) roll back quickly when an optimization change causes a latency/5xx regression. This change introduces an explicit chart-level mechanism to pass the pinned version to the control plane/runtime, making the procedure consistent with the Safe Apply + Rollback project. What’s included: (1) New values: optimize.optimizationConfig.versionPin (string, optional) and optimize.optimizationConfig.allowUnpinned (bool, default true). When versionPin is set, the deployment will request that version from the Optimization Config Service and treat it as the active version for the configured scope. (2) Templating: inject OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_VERSION_PIN and OPTIMIZATION_CONFIG_ALLOW_UNPINNED into the control-plane and runtime components; add checksum annotations so updating values triggers rollouts. (3) Validation: fail chart rendering when versionPin is set but empty/whitespace; add a warning note in NOTES.txt when allowUnpinned=false and no pin is supplied. (4) Docs: add a dedicated “Rollback optimization config” section with a copy-paste example (helm upgrade --set optimize.optimizationConfig.versionPin=<prior_version>) and post-rollback verification steps (check rollout status, confirm config_version tag on metrics, confirm p95 recovery). Backwards compatibility: Existing installs that do not set versionPin behave as before (control plane uses the currently-promoted version / unpinned resolution). No changes to defaults that would impact current clusters. title: Expose optimization config version pinning via Helm values + document rollback procedure

source.title: Add perf-canary Helm chart (safe defaults + region/env config)
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.body:
Summary: This PR adds a first-class Helm chart for the perf-canary service, with conservative defaults intended for initial prod rollout (single region, small model set) and guardrails to prevent accidental wide fanout / excess GPU overhead. The chart supports per-environment and per-region configuration via values + explicit allowlists, and ships with security hardening defaults (non-root, RO filesystem, restricted SA by default) and observability integration hooks.

Context / why: perf-canary needs to be deployed consistently across regions with repeatable config (QPS caps, capacity budget, target models, label set). We also want a safe-disable/rollback path for on-call. This chart is the deployment substrate referenced by ENG-4879 and will be rolled out initially in us-east with a limited model list (ENG-4880).

Key behaviors / defaults:
- Disabled-by-default: chart does not run unless perfCanary.enabled=true.
- Single-region guardrail: requires region to be set explicitly; optionally enforces a regionAllowlist check.
- Capacity safety: defaults target <0.5% overhead via conservative qps + maxInflight limits (tunable).
- Pod hardening: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile=RuntimeDefault, drop all caps, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false.
- Metrics: ServiceMonitor template (Prometheus Operator) + standard labels; histogram buckets controlled via values to avoid high cardinality.
- Rollback/disable: all resources are namespaced; disabling the release cleanly removes canary traffic generation without touching inference endpoints.

What’s included:
- charts/perf-canary (new): Chart.yaml, values.yaml, templates for Deployment, ServiceAccount, ConfigMap, NetworkPolicy, PDB, Service, ServiceMonitor, and helper templates.
- region/env configuration: values supports env, region, tier (hosted/dedicated/private), and an explicit model target list.
- secret wiring: supports referencing an existing secret for canary auth token (created out-of-band by terraform/IAM).

How to deploy (example): helm upgrade --install perf-canary charts/perf-canary -n runtime-canary --create-namespace -f values/prod/us-east.yaml --set perfCanary.enabled=true

Testing:
- chart-testing (ct) lint + install on kind
- helm lint + template render with representative prod values

Rollout notes:
- For phase 1: use us-east only, 2-3 models, warnings only. Paging is handled in observability-pack once thresholds are tuned.

Follow-ups:
- Add a small "values schema" (values.schema.json) for stronger validation and to prevent accidental enabling without region/model list.
- Wire default Grafana dashboard links into annotations once the dashboard UID is finalized.
commits: perf-canary chart skeleton (Chart.yaml/values.yaml) + helpers
Add Deployment/Service templates with securityContext + resource requests
Add ConfigMap rendering for workload profile + region/env config
Add ServiceMonitor + standard labels/annotations
Add guardrails: required region, optional allowlist, fanout limit
Docs: example values and install instructions
review_conversation: Allison Grant: Please ensure the default resource requests are conservative and that we have a clear on-call disable switch. Also prefer RO filesystem + non-root by default.

Connor O'Brien (author): Updated defaults to 50m/128Mi for the control loop container, set runAsNonRoot+RO filesystem, and added NOTES.txt with disable instructions (set enabled=false and helm upgrade).
Sean Gallagher: Can we prevent someone from accidentally deploying this across all regions? The values pattern sometimes gets copied and pasted.

Connor O'Brien (author): Added requiredValues guard for .Values.perfCanary.region and an optional regionAllowlist. Also added a fanoutLimit that fails template rendering if model list > N unless explicitly overridden.
Noah Patel: ConfigMap should include the workload profile knobs we expect (prompt buckets, maxOutputTokens, concurrency). Also want to be sure we don’t accidentally log prompts via env vars.

Connor O'Brien (author): ConfigMap now includes workloadProfile fields and only stores shape metadata (no prompt text). The chart does not accept raw prompt strings; the service generates synthetic prompts internally from a seed + shape config.
Logan Wright: ServiceMonitor labels should match observability-pack conventions; keep label cardinality tight (model/version labels are emitted by the service, not by k8s labels).
Connor O'Brien (author): Adjusted ServiceMonitor selector labels to match conventions and removed "model" from pod labels; model stays in metrics emitted by the canary with a controlled label set.
Vanessa Ortiz: Please ensure we can point at an existing Secret for the auth token and don’t create it in-chart.
Connor O'Brien (author): Added perfCanary.existingSecretName and perfCanary.existingSecretKey; the chart does not create secrets.
source.release_notes: Adds the perf-canary Helm chart with safe defaults, explicit region/env configuration, and Prometheus ServiceMonitor support. Chart is disabled by default and supports referencing an existing Secret for canary auth.

source.title: Fix offline install values.schema.json validation (relax required fields + add defaults)
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.description: Context: During the air-gapped pilots we hit schema validation failures on install/upgrade when using the “minimal offline” values files. The charts were technically installable, but values.schema.json was rejecting inputs due to (a) required fields that are optional in offline mode, and (b) missing defaults for fields that the templates already handle safely. This showed up most often when customers copy only the offline overrides from the docs/runbook (no hosted defaults), and when values are injected by the on-prem installer. What changed: 1) Add safe defaults for common offline paths: - global.airgapped defaults to false - image.pullPolicy defaults to IfNotPresent (offline-friendly) - imagePullSecrets defaults to [] (instead of requiring a non-empty array) - serviceAccount.create defaults to true where appropriate (templates assumed default) 2) Relax overly strict required lists: - Do not require global.imageRegistry in offline mode; accept empty/omitted and allow per-component registry overrides - Do not require external integrations that are optional (metrics exporters, audit log forwarding, etc.) 3) Fix schema typing/oneOf for fields that are frequently set via env var expansion: - Allow integer OR string for select port fields (helm template casts anyway) - Allow "" for optional file/path fields (some customers set empty strings to disable) 4) Tighten only where it matters (kept strict): - Keep additionalProperties: false for the top-level to catch typos - Keep required checks for critical security toggles when enabled (e.g., when audit export is enabled, require destination + credentials refs) Notes: This is intended to unblock offline installs without loosening security-sensitive fields. The signed offline bundle manifest still pins chart versions; this PR only affects schema validation at install time. Testing: - Added a chart-testing job that runs helm lint + schema validation against: a) full default values b) minimal offline values c) values with common SE overrides (custom registry, private CA bundle path, proxy disabled). - Reproduced the pilot failure locally using the same values snippet from SUP-31892 and verified install proceeds. Follow-ups: We should align the docs examples to only include keys that are actually needed; schema changes here are a safety net, not a substitute for clearer guidance.
commits: charts(redwood-private): add defaults for offline-friendly values and relax required list
charts: fix values.schema.json oneOf typing for ports and optional paths
tests: add schema-validation fixtures for minimal offline values
docs: update comments in values.yaml to match schema expectations
changed_files: README.md (developer notes for running schema checks)
conversation: Sahana Iyer (review): The defaults look good, but can we confirm we’re not allowing a blank registry when offline mode is false? If a customer sets global.airgapped=false and omits global.imageRegistry, we should still fail in our hosted-like path.

Dylan Brooks (author): Yep — I constrained the relaxed path using a oneOf on global.airgapped. When airgapped=false we still require either global.imageRegistry or per-component registry overrides. Added a fixture test to cover this.

Connor O'Brien (review): Please add a short note to CHANGELOG about why schema changed; release train needs a heads-up since this is tied to offline bundles. Also, can we link the support ticket for traceability?
Dylan Brooks (author): Added CHANGELOG entry and included SUP-31892 in the PR description + commit message trailer.
Bruno Silva (review): In customer envs we saw empty string used to disable optional file mounts. Schema currently rejects "" for tls.caBundlePath. Can we allow empty string and treat it as unset?
Dylan Brooks (author): Updated schema to allow "" for the optional path fields and confirmed templates already guard with `if` checks. Added fixture `empty-path-disable-values.yaml`.

Sean Gallagher (review): Any risk that relaxing required fields hides typos during install? I like additionalProperties:false at top-level; can we keep it but allow component-specific blocks to accept unknown keys?

Dylan Brooks (author): Kept additionalProperties:false at top-level and in the main component blocks. That should keep typo detection. Where we had to allow flexibility, I added explicit properties rather than opening additionalProperties.
Sahana Iyer (approval): Looks good with the new fixture; thanks for keeping strictness where security toggles apply.
Connor O'Brien (approval): Approved; please merge ASAP so it lands before the next Private bundle cut.
source.release_notes: Private/offline: Helm chart schema validation no longer blocks minimal air-gapped installs; added defaults and relaxed non-critical required fields while preserving strict validation for security-sensitive configuration.

source.title: Align chart versions and appVersion with Private offline bundle manifest metadata
source.author: Devon Price
release_notes: Helm chart packaging now stamps Chart.yaml (version/appVersion) from the signed offline bundle manifest values to prevent mismatches during air-gapped installs.
Release tooling fails early if chart version/appVersion drift from the bundle manifest.
source.body:
Problem: For Redwood Private air-gapped installs, we now ship signed offline bundles where the bundle manifest is the source of truth for versioning. During pilot runs we hit cases where Chart.yaml appVersion drifted from the bundle manifest releaseVersion, which confused support triage and (worse) created ambiguity about what exactly was verified/signed. This PR makes the bundle manifest drive Helm chart versioning/appVersion consistently and adds a CI gate so we fail closed before publishing offline chart tarballs.

Context: Offline bundle signing effort (deterministic manifest + signature verification in installer) requires all user-facing version identifiers to be consistent across: (1) bundle manifest metadata, (2) chart metadata, (3) private release tooling outputs. This PR focuses on (2) and the glue in our chart packaging jobs.

What changed: - Introduced a small version stamping step that reads `private-bundle-manifest.json` (generated by release tooling) and stamps Chart.yaml fields: `version` and `appVersion` - Enforced that `appVersion` == `manifest.releaseVersion` (e.g., `2026.1.0`) across all Private-facing charts - Enforced that Chart `version` is derived deterministically from the manifest as well (we use `manifest.chartVersion`, which is produced by the release train with the same build id) - Updated our chart packaging pipeline to fail if the manifest is missing or if stamping would change a dirty working tree (prevents accidental publishing from non-deterministic state) - Updated chart-testing config to run `helm lint` after stamping so CI matches what we publish

Why this approach: - Keeps a single source of truth: the signed bundle manifest is already what the installer verifies. Chart metadata must match it, not the other way around. - Avoids manual edits at release time. - Makes drift obvious and actionable in CI.

Details / invariants: - `Chart.yaml: appVersion` MUST equal `manifest.releaseVersion` - `Chart.yaml: version` MUST equal `manifest.chartVersion` (semver) - The stamping step is deterministic and produces no changes if run twice with the same inputs - For non-Private charts (dev/test helpers) we do not stamp; only charts included in the Private bundle artifact list

Files / areas touched (high level): - `charts/redwood-platform/Chart.yaml` - `charts/redwood-runtime/Chart.yaml` - `charts/redwood-observability/Chart.yaml` - `scripts/stamp-chart-versions.sh` (new) - `.github/workflows/package-offline-charts.yml` (updated to stamp + verify clean tree) - `ct.yaml` / chart-testing invocation updates

Illustrative snippet (stamping logic): - Reads manifest values via `jq` (pinned in CI image) - Uses `yq` to set fields and preserve formatting - Asserts required fields exist and are non-empty - Exits non-zero if `git diff --quiet` after a second stamping run fails (non-determinism guard)

Testing: - CI: chart-testing, helm lint, packaging job dry-run - Manual: ran `scripts/stamp-chart-versions.sh --manifest ./out/private-bundle-manifest.json` against a local release candidate manifest and confirmed Chart.yaml fields match manifest and are stable across runs

Rollout notes: - Release Engineering will start passing the manifest into chart packaging for the next Private RC - This pairs with PR-18302 (offline chart tarballs + checksums) and installer-side manifest verification work (redwood-private-installer)

Links: - Linear: ENG-4828 (Helm charts offline packaging alignment), ENG-4821 (manifest schema/canonicalization) - Related: redwood-private-installer verification preflight work (bundle manifest is signed; charts must match)

Review conversation highlights:

- Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): "Please make sure we fail if the manifest is missing; we should never publish unstamped charts for Private." 
  - Devon Price: Done. Packaging workflow now requires `PRIVATE_BUNDLE_MANIFEST_PATH` and fails fast with a clear message.

- Jordan Lee: "Can you clarify the invariant between chart version and appVersion? Some tooling assumes chart version increments independently." 
  - Devon Price: Added explicit invariants + rationale in script header and updated workflow comments. For Private, we treat both as release-train outputs.

- Sahana Iyer: "CI should mirror what the release job does. Are we stamping before helm lint?" 
  - Devon Price: Updated: stamping occurs before lint and packaging in both CI and release job.

- Ethan Park: "Any risk this breaks existing customers who pin chart versions?" 
  - Devon Price: For Private offline bundles, customers install via bundle content (not pulling from a public repo). The version displayed will now match the signed bundle metadata, which is desirable. No change to chart templates/values.

CI status: - Initial run failed: `helm lint` on `redwood-observability` due to a schema warning surfaced by chart-testing after stamping (was previously hidden because lint ran pre-stamp). Fixed by updating the chart metadata to include `kubeVersion` constraint consistent with our supported matrix. - Final run: All checks green (chart-testing, lint, package dry-run).

Merge outcome: Merged via squash to keep history clean for release tooling changes.

Files / areas touched (high level): - `charts/redwood-platform/Chart.yaml` - `charts/redwood-runtime/Chart.yaml` - `charts/redwood-observability/Chart.yaml` - `scripts/stamp-chart-versions.sh` (new) - `.github/workflows/package-offline-charts.yml` (updated to stamp + verify clean tree) - `ct.yaml` / chart-testing invocation updates

Illustrative snippet (stamping logic): - Reads manifest values via `jq` (pinned in CI image) - Uses `yq` to set fields and preserve formatting - Asserts required fields exist and are non-empty - Exits non-zero if `git diff --quiet` after a second stamping run fails (non-determinism guard)

  - Devon Price: Updated: stamping occurs before lint and packaging in both CI and release job.

Fixed by updating the chart metadata to include `kubeVersion` constraint consistent with our supported matrix. - Final run: All checks green (chart-testing, lint, package dry-run).

source.title: Add Helm values for hardware tuning profile pack + override controls
source.author: Diego Santos
source.release_notes: Helm charts now support configuring the hardware tuning profile pack (enable/disable, pack version/source) and explicit override behavior for Dedicated/Private deployments. Defaults are safe (profiles disabled unless explicitly enabled; version pin required for external bundles).
source.body: Title: Add Helm values for hardware tuning profile pack + override controls Context: Runtime now supports loading a versioned hardware tuning profile pack (H100/B200/L40S) and selecting an effective profile based on GPU SKU + deployment overrides (see runtime PRs: redwood/pr-21991, redwood/pr-22027). Dedicated/Private installs need a supported way to (a) ship the profile pack bundle alongside the runtime, (b) pin the pack version, and (c) control override order without requiring ad-hoc ConfigMap edits that drift from supported configs. What this PR does: Wires profile pack configuration into the redwood-runtime chart values and templates, adds docs for operators, and keeps backward compatibility with existing runtimeConfig.* values. Key behavior is intentionally conservative: profiles remain OFF by default and the chart will not change runtime behavior unless the operator explicitly enables the profile pack. Summary of changes (operator-facing): 1) New values under runtime.hardwareProfiles.* (Dedicated/Private): - runtime.hardwareProfiles.enabled (default: false) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.version (required when enabled for external sources; optional for builtin) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.source: "builtin" | "configMap" | "inline" (default: "builtin") - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.configMapName (used when source=configMap) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.inlineJson (used when source=inline; intended for testing only) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.allowEnvOverrides (default: true) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.allowHelmValueOverrides (default: true) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.lockProfileName (default: ""; when set, pins a specific profile name and disables auto-detect) - runtime.hardwareProfiles.override.precedence (default: "env,helm,fleet,autodetect") 2) Chart templates mount the pack and plumb settings to runtime via env vars/args, consistent with the runtime loader’s precedence rules: Effective config is determined in this order by default: (1) explicit env overrides (intended for oncall / emergency mitigation), then (2) Helm-provided overrides (values.yaml / per-install), then (3) fleet defaults (if present in private-control-plane integration), then (4) GPU SKU autodetect (H100/B200/L40S) with safe fallback to "generic". If the runtime cannot safely detect SKU or the pack/version is incompatible, it falls back to runtime defaults and emits a startup log warning (no crash-loop by default). 3) Adds documentation: - docs/README.md section: “Hardware tuning profile pack” - values.yaml examples for: Dedicated H100 pool, Private install w/ ConfigMap bundle, emergency disable via env var - explicit wording around “expected behavior” vs guarantees (aligns with SRE feedback) Notes on safe defaults / compatibility: - enabled=false by default to avoid unexpected perf/latency changes on upgrade. - When enabled and source=configMap, version pin is required to avoid silent drift. - If lockProfileName is set, chart renders a validation warning in NOTES.txt if enabled but no profile is found at startup (runtime-level). - B200 remains experimental; this chart does not auto-enable B200-specific profile—operators must opt-in by enabling profiles and allowing autodetect/lockProfileName as appropriate (runtime gating remains enforced by runtime feature flag and compatibility checks). Implementation details: - Adds templates/hardware-profiles-configmap.yaml when runtime.hardwareProfiles.pack.source=inline (test/dev only) - Adds volume+mount for configMap source and sets RUNTIME_PROFILE_PACK_PATH for runtime - Adds env wiring for: RUNTIME_HARDWARE_PROFILES_ENABLED, RUNTIME_PROFILE_PACK_VERSION, RUNTIME_PROFILE_OVERRIDE_PRECEDENCE, RUNTIME_PROFILE_LOCK_NAME, RUNTIME_PROFILE_ALLOW_ENV_OVERRIDES - Updates templates/NOTES.txt to include operator guidance + quick verification commands (kubectl exec + runtime debug endpoint) Testing: - helm lint and chart unit tests updated - Verified rendered manifests for: (a) Dedicated with no profiles (no changes vs prior render), (b) Private with configMap bundle, (c) inline bundle smoke for CI - Manual sanity: runtime starts when bundle missing (enabled=false), and fails “softly” (warn + fallback) when enabled but bundle path invalid (relies on runtime behavior; no crash loop introduced by chart). Rollout: - This is required for the staged profile pack rollout in Dedicated/Private (ENG-4830). Hosted will consume pack internally and does not use this chart. Follow-ups: - Consider adding a chart-level schema check once runtime schema keys are finalized (will land after ENG-4822 stabilizes). - Add a helper to generate ConfigMap from a profile pack tarball for Private operators (separate tooling task).
changed_areas: charts/redwood-runtime/README.md (hardware profiles section, override order, examples)
.github/workflows/chart-ci.yml (adds a render case with profiles enabled via inline bundle)
commits: Add runtime.hardwareProfiles values and comments to values.yaml
Template: mount profile pack bundle and wire env vars/args
Docs: describe override order, safe defaults, and Private/Dedicated examples
Tests: update golden renders + add profile-enabled render case
review_conversation: Keiko Yamamoto: Can we make sure enabling profiles doesn’t change autoscaling by default? Some pools have tight memory headroom. Diego Santos: Yes—this PR only wires the pack + override controls. Autoscaling knobs remain unchanged. Also left enabled=false default to avoid surprises.

Ethan Park: Precedence string feels fragile; can we constrain to known tokens? Diego Santos: Added docs with allowed tokens and a NOTES.txt warning; runtime validates and falls back to default precedence if malformed (as per loader implementation).

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Please add an explicit emergency disable path that doesn’t require Helm upgrade during incident. Diego Santos: Added allowEnvOverrides=true default and documented setting RUNTIME_HARDWARE_PROFILES_ENABLED=0 as the fastest mitigation; also added NOTES.txt snippet.

Nina Petrova: ConfigMap name should be namespaced/predictable to avoid collisions in multi-release namespaces. Diego Santos: Kept it operator-provided (configMapName), but for inline source the generated ConfigMap uses {{ include "redwood-runtime.fullname" }}-hardware-profiles to remain per-release unique.

Connor O'Brien: Any upgrade notes? If someone upgrades chart but had previously hacked in a mount, will it break? Diego Santos: Added a doc note: the new values are optional; existing custom mounts still work, but we recommend moving to supported keys. No template behavior changes unless enabled=true.
source.notes_for_oncall: If a regression is suspected after enabling profiles in a Dedicated/Private pool, set env var RUNTIME_HARDWARE_PROFILES_ENABLED=0 (or set runtime.hardwareProfiles.enabled=false and redeploy). Verify selected profile via runtime metrics (profile_selected, profile_override_source) and/or debug endpoint as documented in NOTES.txt.

source.title: Publish offline chart tarballs with sha256 checksums for signed Private bundles
source.author: Devon Price
source.release_notes: Private/on-prem offline bundle pipeline can now consume published chart tarballs and verify their sha256 hashes from a generated checksums file (referenced by the signed bundle manifest).
source.body: Summary: Adds a deterministic packaging step for Redwood Helm charts intended for Redwood Private offline bundles. The pipeline now produces versioned chart tarballs (.tgz) for each supported chart and emits a sha256 checksums file that can be referenced by the signed offline bundle manifest. Motivation: For air-gapped installs, we need to transport a complete set of Helm charts without relying on remote chart repos. Bundle signing (separate work in the installer/release pipeline) needs stable inputs: a predictable chart archive name + a hash file that can be included in the bundle manifest and signed. This PR does NOT implement signing here; it only produces the chart artifacts and checksum outputs in a stable layout for the release tooling to ingest. Key changes: (1) New make target `make package-offline` that runs `helm dependency build` with pinned versions, packages charts to `dist/offline-charts/<channel>/<version>/`, and writes `checksums-sha256.txt` in the same directory. (2) Standardized archive naming: `<chartName>-<chartVersion>.tgz` (no build metadata). (3) Deterministic packaging: sets `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` (when present) and normalizes file ordering before hashing to reduce drift across runs; also ensures `Chart.lock` is generated and included in the packaged chart. (4) Adds CI job `offline-chart-artifacts` that runs packaging and verifies that rerunning packaging yields identical checksums within the same commit (best-effort determinism check; see notes). (5) Documentation: updates `RELEASING.md` with the offline artifact path conventions and how the signing/manifest tooling should reference the output. Output format: `dist/offline-charts/private/<appVersion>/` contains `redwood-private-<chartVersion>.tgz`, `redwood-runtime-<chartVersion>.tgz`, `redwood-observability-<chartVersion>.tgz`, plus `checksums-sha256.txt`. The checksums file uses one entry per line: `<sha256>  <filename>` (two spaces) to match common tooling (`sha256sum -c`). Example: `8d2b2a6c9b0f5d2bb8e1c7f2e7bb8f1d74b6b9b3f71a4f5cd2d2f7b1c4a9f3d2  redwood-private-1.18.0.tgz`. Notes / determinism constraints: Helm chart packaging can still drift if dependencies pull non-deterministic archives or if the local environment injects timestamps. We mitigate most of this by requiring dependencies to be pinned, generating `Chart.lock`, and hashing the final tarballs. CI checks that the checksum file remains stable on a second packaging pass in the same runner. If we see drift across runners, release tooling should treat the checksums as the source of truth for a given build id and record the build provenance in the signed bundle manifest (tracked in ENG-4822/installer work). Files/areas changed (high-level): `.github/workflows/offline-chart-artifacts.yml` (new CI job), `Makefile` (new packaging targets), `scripts/package_offline_charts.sh` (new; creates dist layout and checksums), `charts/*/Chart.yaml` (minor alignment + ensure dependencies are pinned), `RELEASING.md` (offline artifact conventions). Commits: 1) chore(ci): add workflow to build offline chart artifacts and upload as build artifact 2) build: add package-offline target + packaging script 3) build: generate checksums-sha256.txt and validate via sha256sum -c 4) docs: update releasing guide with offline bundle chart artifact layout Review conversation: Connor O'Brien: Can we guarantee the output paths match what the Private bundle manifest generator expects? Devon Price: Added explicit directory layout in RELEASING.md and aligned with the current bundle tooling draft; also added env vars `OFFLINE_CHART_CHANNEL` + `OFFLINE_CHART_VERSION` so release tooling can control the exact destination. Connor O'Brien: Please ensure the checksum file format is compatible with `sha256sum -c` on minimal distros. Devon Price: Updated to two-space separator; added a CI smoke check using `sha256sum -c checksums-sha256.txt`. Sahana Iyer: The determinism check is good, but can we fail if `helm dependency update` runs? We shouldn’t be updating dependencies during packaging. Devon Price: Packaging script now uses `helm dependency build` and errors if `Chart.lock` would change (git diff check). Jordan Lee: Please add an example stanza for how the manifest should reference these outputs (relative paths). Devon Price: Added a short example in RELEASING.md showing `charts/redwood-private-<ver>.tgz` and `charts/checksums-sha256.txt` entries as inputs to the manifest builder. Ethan Park: Any risk to existing online chart publishing? Devon Price: No changes to chart repo publishing; this is additive and only runs in the new CI workflow and release tooling. CI: build/lint (pass), chart lint (pass), offline-chart-artifacts (pass), determinism re-run check (pass). Merge: Squash merged by Connor O'Brien after approvals. Follow-ups: ENG-4828 will wire these artifacts into the offline bundle builder; installer PRs will verify hashes from the signed manifest during preflight.

We mitigate most of this by requiring dependencies to be pinned, generating `Chart.lock`, and hashing the final tarballs. Devon Price: Packaging script now uses `helm dependency build` and errors if `Chart.lock` would change (git diff check). source.title: Add optional workload annotations/labels for offline bundle build correlation
source.author: Devon Price
source.description: Context: For Redwood Private air-gapped installs we now ship signed offline bundles. Support needs a deterministic way to correlate a running cluster (workloads) to the exact offline bundle build that installed it, without requiring customers to upload the full bundle or logs. This PR adds optional annotations/labels to charts so the installer (or operators) can inject a bundle build identifier into Kubernetes objects. Summary: (1) Add a shared helper that renders bundle metadata into standard annotations/labels, (2) plumb through values in each chart with no-op defaults, and (3) document the values and ensure templates don’t change existing output unless the values are set. Details: - New values under global.offlineBundle (all optional): buildId (string), releaseId (string), manifestDigest (string), signerKeyId (string). - New standard keys used across workloads: annotations: redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id, redwood.ai/offline-bundle-release-id, redwood.ai/offline-bundle-manifest-digest, redwood.ai/offline-bundle-signer-key-id; labels: redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id (kept minimal to avoid label bloat and label length constraints). - All keys are only emitted when the corresponding value is non-empty. - Values are added to: Deployment/StatefulSet pod template metadata + top-level object metadata for easier kubectl queries, and to Jobs/CronJobs where present. - Added a small NOTES.txt section to hint how to query: kubectl get pods -A -l redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id=<id>. Motivation: This is the glue needed for support bundles and for triaging verification/upgrade issues (e.g., confirming a cluster is on a specific signed bundle build when the customer can’t export full logs). This aligns with the broader signed offline bundle effort and downstream work in the installer to record/emit build IDs. Testing: helm lint, template snapshot tests for each chart verifying no diff when values unset; new golden tests verifying keys appear when set; validated against a kind cluster by installing with global.offlineBundle.buildId=rbwpriv_2025-11-18. Follow-ups: The installer PR will set these values automatically from the signed manifest/build metadata; docs PR to include recommended query patterns for air-gapped support cases.
commits: charts: add offline bundle metadata helper and values schema
charts: wire helper into runtime + gateway templates
charts: add template snapshot tests for bundle metadata keys
docs: update NOTES + values reference for offline bundle metadata
source.ci_summary: pass: helm-lint, unit-tests (template snapshots), chart-packaging (dry-run), docs-check; no retries
review_conversation: Ethan Park: Can we keep label usage minimal? We’ve hit label length/selector constraints in a few customer clusters. Also please ensure we don’t change rendered manifests when values are empty.
Devon Price (author): Yep—labels will only include build-id (short, installer-controlled). All other fields are annotations only. Added snapshot tests verifying no manifest diffs when unset.
Connor O'Brien: Naming: prefer redwood.ai/offline-bundle-build-id vs bundle-build-id for clarity. Also can we add signer key id for post-rotation troubleshooting?
Devon Price (author): Updated keys to redwood.ai/offline-bundle-*, added signerKeyId as annotation only, and documented it in values.yaml + NOTES.
Logan Wright: If we’re going to rely on this in support bundles, we should put it both on pod template metadata and top-level metadata so we can query workloads and pods. Is that covered?
Devon Price (author): Added to both object metadata and pod template metadata for Deployment/StatefulSet/Job/CronJob templates.
Kira Thompson: Please add a short NOTES snippet with the kubectl query so support and customers have a ready command.
Devon Price (author): Added NOTES section with kubectl examples and note that keys appear only when values are set.
Ethan Park: Looks good now. Approved.
Connor O'Brien: Approved; please link this PR in the installer rollout checklist.
Devon Price (author): Linked to ENG-4828 and added note in release train thread.
release_notes: Helm charts: optional annotations/labels can be set to record the offline bundle build ID on Kubernetes workloads for support correlation (no-op unless configured).

source.title: Add node validation agent DaemonSet (RBAC, resources, configurable checks)
source.author: Gabe Wilson
source.description:
## Summary
Adds a new Helm chart `node-validation-agent` that deploys the Provision-to-Ready node validation agent as a DaemonSet.

This chart is used by Fleet automation to run post-provision validation gates on GPU worker nodes before they are marked `Ready` in the control plane.

Key features:
- DaemonSet deployment with sane defaults for GPU nodes (tolerations/nodeSelector/priorityClass)
- Least-privilege RBAC (read-only access to Node + Events; optional Lease for heartbeat)
- Resource requests/limits and safe pod security context
- Configurable validation checks (enable/disable + per-check timeouts) via ConfigMap
- Optional OTLP export (disabled by default) + structured logs to stdout

## Why
Provision-to-ready automation needs a consistent, repeatable way to run the same validations on every node across regions and GPU SKUs. Shipping the agent as a chart lets us:
- pin versions alongside cluster config
- roll out by region/pool with existing Helm pipelines
- standardize security posture and RBAC

## What’s included
### Chart
- `charts/node-validation-agent/Chart.yaml`
- `charts/node-validation-agent/values.yaml`
- `charts/node-validation-agent/templates/{daemonset,serviceaccount,rbac,configmap}.yaml`
- `values.schema.json` for basic validation

### Default checks
Defaults are conservative (fast + actionable). NCCL smoke test remains off by default for now (flake tuning in progress).

Enabled by default:
- `gpu_health` (ECC/Xid scan, basic CUDA device enumeration)
- `driver_parity` (driver/CUDA/NCCL versions vs expected matrix)
- `network_smoke` (MTU + basic throughput probe to same-AZ endpoint)
- `storage_smoke` (disk layout + minimal IOPS sanity)
- `runtime_smoke` (container runtime + nvidia runtime presence)

Disabled by default:
- `nccl_smoke` (can be enabled per pool/region)

## Values (highlights)
```yaml
image:
  repository: ghcr.io/redwood-inference/node-validation-agent
  tag: "0.6.0"
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

agent:
  checks:
    gpu_health:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 60
    driver_parity:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 60
    network_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    storage_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    runtime_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    nccl_smoke:
      enabled: false
      timeoutSeconds: 180

  expected:
    # populated by fleet automation per pool (values override)
    nvidiaDriverMajor: "550"
    cudaMinor: "12.4"

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi

rbac:
  create: true
  # if true, allows agent to create/update a Lease for heartbeat in its namespace
  enableLeaseHeartbeat: false

telemetry:
  otlp:
    enabled: false
    endpoint: ""

nodeSelector:
  redwood.ai/node-type: gpu-worker

tolerations:
  - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"

priorityClassName: "system-node-critical"
```

## Security / permissions
- Runs as non-root by default (`runAsUser: 65534`, `runAsNonRoot: true`).
- `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
- Drops all capabilities.
- No host networking.

GPU inspection notes:
- The agent executes `nvidia-smi` by mounting it from the host (`/usr/bin/nvidia-smi`) and mounts `/proc/driver/nvidia` read-only. This avoids bundling driver tooling in the container and keeps the container image small.
- Does not request `nvidia.com/gpu` resources (so it doesn’t reserve a GPU). It only reads health/driver state.

## Operational notes
- Uses a checksum annotation on the ConfigMap so edits trigger a rollout.
- DaemonSet update strategy is `RollingUpdate` with `maxUnavailable: 10%` (tuned in review).
- `terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 10` to avoid hanging during drain.

## Testing
- `helm template` + `helm lint` in CI
- Rendered manifests applied to a dev GPU pool (us-east, A100) via staging pipeline
- Verified that pods schedule on tainted GPU nodes and emit expected JSON logs

## Rollout
This chart will be installed only in staging for now. Fleet automation will enable it behind the staged rollout flag per pool (separate PR in control plane/terraform).
commits: charts(node-validation-agent): initial chart scaffolding + default values
templates: DaemonSet with securityContext, tolerations, updateStrategy
rbac: ClusterRole/Binding for node reads; optional Lease permission
config: ConfigMap for enabled checks + timeouts; checksum rollout annotation
ci: add chart linting and schema validation
source.review_conversation:
Vanessa Ortiz: Can we make the check list explicitly configurable per pool, and ensure we can disable NCCL by default? Also please add a comment about why we’re mounting nvidia-smi from host.

Gabe Wilson (author): Updated values to be per-check enable/disable with timeouts. NCCL stays disabled by default. Added docs in values.yaml + README explaining host mount approach.

Rafael Mendes: RBAC looks a bit broad. Do we really need write permissions anywhere? Also RollingUpdate maxUnavailable=1 will take forever on large pools.

Gabe Wilson (author): RBAC is now read-only by default (get/list/watch nodes + create events removed). Added `rbac.enableLeaseHeartbeat` toggle; only when enabled does it grant Lease create/update in the release namespace. Updated updateStrategy to `maxUnavailable: 10%` (overrideable).

Fiona McCarthy: Please enforce `readOnlyRootFilesystem`, drop caps, and ensure we don’t run privileged. Also confirm the hostPath mounts are read-only.

Gabe Wilson (author): Added pod/container securityContext: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false, drop ALL, seccomp=RuntimeDefault. HostPath mounts are now explicitly `readOnly: true`.

Allison Grant: Telemetry: please don’t default OTLP on; also avoid adding high-cardinality labels/annotations.

Gabe Wilson (author): OTLP export remains disabled by default; config uses static keys only. No per-node IDs added to labels.

Approvals: Vanessa Ortiz approved; Rafael Mendes approved; Fiona McCarthy approved (security); Allison Grant approved (observability).
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash merge into main by Vanessa Ortiz.
