```yaml
  checks:
    gpu_health:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 60
    driver_parity:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 60
    network_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    storage_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    runtime_smoke:
      enabled: true
      timeoutSeconds: 45
    nccl_smoke:
      enabled: false
      timeoutSeconds: 180

  expected:
    # populated by fleet automation per pool (values override)
    nvidiaDriverMajor: "550"
    cudaMinor: "12.4"

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi

rbac:
  create: true
  # if true, allows agent to create/update a Lease for heartbeat in its namespace
  enableLeaseHeartbeat: false

telemetry:
  otlp:
    enabled: false
    endpoint: ""

nodeSelector:
  redwood.ai/node-type: gpu-worker

tolerations:
  - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"

priorityClassName: "system-node-critical"
```

## Security / permissions
- Runs as non-root by default (`runAsUser: 65534`, `runAsNonRoot: true`).
- `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
- Drops all capabilities.
- No host networking.

GPU inspection notes:
- The agent executes `nvidia-smi` by mounting it from the host (`/usr/bin/nvidia-smi`) and mounts `/proc/driver/nvidia` read-only. This avoids bundling driver tooling in the container and keeps the container image small.
- Does not request `nvidia.com/gpu` resources (so it doesn’t reserve a GPU). It only reads health/driver state.

## Operational notes
- Uses a checksum annotation on the ConfigMap so edits trigger a rollout.
- DaemonSet update strategy is `RollingUpdate` with `maxUnavailable: 10%` (tuned in review).
- `terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 10` to avoid hanging during drain.

## Testing
- `helm template` + `helm lint` in CI
- Rendered manifests applied to a dev GPU pool (us-east, A100) via staging pipeline
- Verified that pods schedule on tainted GPU nodes and emit expected JSON logs

## Rollout
This chart will be installed only in staging for now. Fleet automation will enable it behind the staged rollout flag per pool (separate PR in control plane/terraform).

source.title: Wire Private observability minimum pack (dashboards + alerts) into Helm values
source.author: Diego Santos
source.description: This PR wires the v1 "Private observability minimum pack" artifacts (Grafana dashboard bundle + Prometheus alert/recording rules) into the `redwood-private` Helm chart values, with customer-friendly toggles and sane defaults. Motivation: today Private installs often require manual copying of dashboards/rules, which makes upgrades brittle (e.g., empty dashboards after upgrade) and makes it harder for Support/SRE to assume a baseline. This change makes the minimum pack installable via `values.yaml` and version-pins the artifact bundle so air-gapped installs can package it reliably. What’s included: (1) Dashboards: ships a versioned dashboard bundle as ConfigMaps that work with the Grafana sidecar pattern. Customers can enable/disable per-category (runtime, control-plane, infra) and choose the label/key used by their Grafana sidecar. (2) Alerts/recording rules: ships `PrometheusRule` resources when the Prometheus Operator CRD is present; otherwise can optionally emit rules as a raw rules ConfigMap for non-operator Prometheus setups. (3) Exporter + retention toggles: adds values to enable/disable the recommended minimum exporters (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, DCGM exporter) when customers choose to deploy the bundled stack, and exposes retention / storage knobs for Prometheus (and Grafana persistence when enabled). Non-goals: this does not introduce "premium" features (federation, long retention defaults, custom dashboards). It also does not force-install Prometheus/Grafana; by default we only ship artifacts and create ServiceMonitors/PrometheusRules when enabled. Values summary (new): `observability.enabled` (default true), `observability.dashboards.enabled` (default true), `observability.dashboards.sidecar.label` (default `grafana_dashboard`), `observability.dashboards.sidecar.folderAnnotation` (default `grafana_folder`), `observability.alerts.enabled` (default true), `observability.alerts.mode` = `prometheusOperator|configMap` (default `prometheusOperator`), `observability.alerts.runbookBaseUrl` (used to stamp alert annotations), `observability.stack.enabled` (default false; if true installs `kube-prometheus-stack` + exporters with bounded defaults), `observability.stack.prometheus.retention` (default `15d`), `observability.stack.prometheus.storage.size` (default `50Gi`), `observability.stack.exporters.kubeStateMetrics.enabled` (default true when stack enabled), `observability.stack.exporters.nodeExporter.enabled` (default true when stack enabled), `observability.stack.exporters.dcgmExporter.enabled` (default true when stack enabled). Implementation notes: dashboards and rules are imported from the `observability-pack` bundle (pinned by version in chart values) and rendered into templates via a small helper that keeps ConfigMaps under the Kubernetes size limits by splitting across multiple ConfigMaps. PrometheusRule creation is gated on both values and CRD presence (`monitoring.coreos.com/v1`). Alert annotations include: summary, description, severity, and `runbook_url` pointing to our internal runbooks (base URL configurable for customer docs mirrors in air-gapped environments). Testing: `helm template` on defaults; `ct lint` for chart schema; kind-based smoke deploy with Grafana sidecar and Prometheus Operator verifying dashboards appear and Prometheus loads rules. Related: ENG-19152 (chart wiring), PRs in `observability-pack` adding the bundles, and installer preflight work in `redwood-private-installer` (separate PR) to validate CRDs/storage prerequisites.
commits: redwood-private: add observability values schema + docs comments
templates: render dashboard bundle into labeled ConfigMaps (split by size)
templates: add PrometheusRule resources + CRD gating + configMap fallback
charts: optional kube-prometheus-stack wiring behind observability.stack.enabled
alerts: stamp runbook_url annotations from configurable base URL
ci: add chart-testing job to validate new values permutations
source.conversation: Dylan Brooks (review): Can we avoid assuming a specific Grafana sidecar label? Some customers use `grafana_dashboard` but others use `grafana_dashboard: "1"` patterns. Author (Diego): Added `observability.dashboards.sidecar.label` and `observability.dashboards.sidecar.labelValue` (default empty -> just key exists), plus `folderAnnotation` override; updated README comments. Logan Wright (review): PrometheusRule should be gated on CRD presence; we’ve seen installs without operator. Also we need a safe fallback. Author: Added `.Capabilities.APIVersions.Has "monitoring.coreos.com/v1"` gating; introduced `alerts.mode=configMap` that emits a rules ConfigMap (customer can mount into Prometheus) and documented it. Allison Grant (review): Please ensure alert severities map to our Private standards and include runbook URLs. Also recording rules should be separate so they can be disabled if cardinality is an issue. Author: Updated annotations to include `runbook_url` and clarified severity mapping in values comments; added `observability.recordingRules.enabled` toggle (default true) and grouped recording rules into a separate manifest section. Rafael Mendes (review): Default retention/storage for bundled Prometheus feels too low for some clusters and too high for others; can we document sizing guidance and keep defaults conservative? Author: Kept defaults at 15d/50Gi but added explicit values and comments; linked to infra sizing doc in README; also set CPU/mem requests for kube-prometheus-stack components behind the stack flag only. Chloe Martin (review): ConfigMaps can exceed size limits with dashboard JSON. How are we splitting? Author: Added helper to split by dashboard file and chunk by byte limit; validated template output < 900KiB per ConfigMap; added a chart-testing install job with stack enabled + dashboards enabled. Final: Approvals from Dylan, Logan, Allison; Rafael approved after re-run; merged via squash.
release_notes: Private Helm chart now supports installing the minimum supported observability artifacts (Grafana dashboards + Prometheus alerts/recording rules) via values toggles.
Optional bundled Prometheus/Grafana stack can be enabled behind `observability.stack.enabled`, with configurable retention/storage and exporter toggles.
Alert resources include configurable runbook URLs to support customer doc mirrors in air-gapped environments.

source.title: Private: add deterministic pre-upgrade job ordering + timeouts (rollback-safe hooks)
source.author: Irene Choi
source.release_notes: - Private upgrades now run pre-upgrade hooks in a deterministic order with bounded timeouts. - Pre-upgrade jobs are configured to be rollback-safe and avoid leaving stuck hook resources behind.
source.body:
## Summary
This updates Redwood Private Helm hook behavior to make in-place upgrades safer by default.

Key changes:
- Deterministic ordering for pre-upgrade jobs using `helm.sh/hook-weight` (and documentation/guardrails around required weights).
- Explicit, bounded timeouts on pre-upgrade jobs (and on key rollout waits) to avoid indefinite hangs during maintenance windows.
- Rollback-safe hook behaviors: consistent `hook-delete-policy` and job specs tuned to reduce stuck pods/resources.

This PR is part of the “Private upgrade + rollback mechanism” effort and is intended to reduce upgrade-related incidents caused by partially-completed hook execution or unbounded waiting. Related to ENG-4860 (ordering/readiness gates) and surfaced by SUP-18421 (DB migration step could hang and block the entire upgrade).

## Motivation / Problem
In Private upgrades we rely on Helm hooks for a small set of upgrade-critical actions (e.g., preflight/migration checks, schema gate jobs, compatibility checks). Previously:
- Hook execution order depended on chart templating order and could change as files were refactored.
- Some jobs did not have explicit `activeDeadlineSeconds` / `backoffLimit` / `ttlSecondsAfterFinished`, which led to long-running or stuck jobs and ambiguous operator experience.
- Hook resources weren’t consistently cleaned up, which occasionally caused follow-on upgrades to fail due to `AlreadyExists` or stuck pods.

## What changed
### 1) Hook ordering
Standardized hook weights for pre-upgrade jobs:
- `-10`: cluster-level prerequisites (e.g., CRD compatibility checks)
- `0`: DB connectivity / migration eligibility checks
- `10`: schema migration jobs (when used)
- `20`: post-migration verification gates

We intentionally keep the weight scale simple and documented so future additions don’t accidentally interleave with migrations.

### 2) Timeouts and retry semantics
Updated hook jobs to include:
- `activeDeadlineSeconds` (bounded execution)
- `backoffLimit` (bounded retries)
- `ttlSecondsAfterFinished` (cleanup without requiring a manual delete)

Also added chart values for operators to override defaults when needed for unusually large deployments (e.g., very large DBs / slower storage), without requiring a chart patch.

### 3) Rollback-safe hook deletion policy
Normalized hook annotations across affected jobs:
- `helm.sh/hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded`

Rationale:
- `before-hook-creation` prevents `AlreadyExists` when re-running an upgrade after failure.
- `hook-succeeded` keeps the cluster tidy.
- We avoid `hook-failed` deletion by default so operators/SRE can inspect failure logs and artifacts during an incident (consistent with our on-call playbook).

## Operator impact
- Upgrades should fail faster and more deterministically when pre-upgrade checks/migrations cannot complete.
- Re-running an upgrade after a failure should be less likely to get stuck on stale hook resources.
- Default timeouts are conservative; operators can tune via values if their environment requires longer windows.

## Testing
- `ct lint` + `helm lint` for affected charts
- Render tests for hook annotations/weights (ensures we don’t regress ordering)
- Upgrade simulation in kind (smoke): run `helm upgrade` with a deliberately failing pre-upgrade job and verify:
  - upgrade aborts
  - failed hook job remains for inspection
  - subsequent upgrade run deletes/recreates hook job cleanly

## Follow-ups
- ENG-2204 follow-up PR will further separate control-plane and data-plane rollout phases; this PR focuses only on pre-upgrade ordering/timeouts.
- We should add a small doc section to the Private runbook describing where to look for hook job logs and how to tune timeout values (tracked in docs PR).

---

### Changed areas (high level)
- `charts/redwood-private/*`: pre-upgrade hook annotations updated
- `charts/redwood-private/templates/hooks/*`: standardized weights + job timeouts
- `charts/redwood-private/values.yaml`: new knobs for hook job timeouts/retries
- `charts/redwood-private/README.md`: ordering and operator guidance

### Illustrative snippet
Updated job template (representative):
- Added `helm.sh/hook-weight` and `helm.sh/hook-delete-policy`
- Added `activeDeadlineSeconds`, `backoffLimit`, and `ttlSecondsAfterFinished`

No application-level logic changes are included here; this is chart behavior only.

## Checklist
- [x] Deterministic hook weights for all pre-upgrade jobs
- [x] Bounded timeouts and retries
- [x] Rollback-safe hook deletion policy
- [x] CI green
- [x] Linked incident/tickets
commits: charts(private): standardize pre-upgrade hook weights
charts(private): add activeDeadlineSeconds/backoffLimit/ttlSecondsAfterFinished to hook jobs
charts(private): normalize hook-delete-policy for rollback-safe reruns
docs(private): document hook ordering + timeout overrides
tests: add render assertions for hook annotations/weights
conversation: Connor O'Brien (Release Eng) 2025-05-07: Can we avoid deleting failed hook jobs? We need logs for support bundles and upgrade forensics.
Irene Choi (author) 2025-05-07: Agreed. Defaulting to `before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded`. Not adding `hook-failed` so failed jobs remain inspectable. Added note in README.
Sean Gallagher (SRE) 2025-05-08: Please make sure timeouts are bounded but configurable; some customers have slower storage/DBs. Also ensure we don’t accidentally reorder migration vs verification.
Irene Choi (author) 2025-05-09: Added values for `preUpgradeJobs.activeDeadlineSeconds` and per-job overrides. Weights documented and tested via render assertions.
Victor Alvarez (Networking) 2025-05-10: Any hook that depends on DNS/TLS should have clear failure output. Are we surfacing that here?
Irene Choi (author) 2025-05-10: Not changing job logic in this PR, but ensured failed jobs stick around so operators can `kubectl logs`. Follow-up is in installer precheck UX.
Rafael Mendes (On-call) 2025-05-12: +1 on keeping failed jobs. Can we ensure rerun cleans up previous successful hooks? We’ve seen `AlreadyExists` once.
Irene Choi (author) 2025-05-12: Yep — `before-hook-creation` handles that. Also added TTL to clean up completed jobs automatically.
Connor O'Brien 2025-05-14: Looks good. Approving with request to keep weight ranges stable going forward.
Sean Gallagher 2025-05-14: Approved. Please coordinate with PR-2204 to avoid conflicts in hook templates.

source.title: Split Redwood Private chart into control-plane + data-plane rollout phases (with readiness gates)
source.author: Irene Choi
source.description:
## Context
Private upgrades need deterministic ordering so we never roll data-plane changes (runtime/model-cache/agent pools) until the control plane is fully upgraded and stable (API/console/authn/z, migrations, webhook controllers).

We previously tried to approximate ordering inside a single Helm release via hook weights, but Helm still applies manifest changes as one transaction. This made it possible to partially roll DP resources even when CP was unhealthy (e.g., CP deployment stuck due to migration lock contention).

This PR introduces a clean separation:
- A **control-plane chart** installed/upgraded first
- A **data-plane chart** installed/upgraded second
- A lightweight **readiness gate** that blocks DP upgrades until CP health + migration state are OK

This matches the installer/orchestrator plan stages being implemented in redwood-private-installer (plan generation + phase execution).

## What changed
### 1) New charts
- `charts/redwood-private-control-plane/`
  - control-plane API, console, auth proxy, admission/webhook controllers, migration job, RBAC/ServiceAccounts, CP services
- `charts/redwood-private-data-plane/`
  - data-plane runtime deployments/daemonsets, model-cache, DP services, HPA/autoscaling primitives

### 2) Umbrella chart changes
- `charts/redwood-private/` is now an umbrella chart that pins versions and wires shared values. It no longer installs CP/DP resources directly.
- Values are split to avoid cross-phase coupling:
  - `controlPlane.*` (required for CP release)
  - `dataPlane.*` (required for DP release)
  - `global.*` remains for shared concerns (image registry overrides, TLS mode, common labels)

### 3) Readiness gate
We add a small job (shipped with the **data-plane** chart) that blocks upgrades/installs until the control plane reports ready and migrations are complete.
- Implemented as a `pre-install,pre-upgrade` hook Job `redwood-cp-readiness-gate`
- Gate checks:
  - Kubernetes readiness: CP deployments are available
  - HTTP readiness: `/readyz` on `control-plane-api` returns 200
  - Migration state: `redwood-migrator` job is complete OR `schema_version` is at/above target (when migrator is disabled)
- Timeouts are configurable via `dataPlane.upgradeGate.timeoutSeconds` (default: 900s)

### 4) Backwards compatibility + upgrade path
This is a packaging change that affects operators using helm directly.
- Installer-managed environments: the installer will now create two helm releases in the correct order.
- Helm-direct users: follow the migration steps in `charts/redwood-private/README.md`.

We keep old values as deprecated aliases where feasible (e.g., `api.*` -> `controlPlane.api.*`) for one release cycle, but release names and chart structure are intentionally changed to make ordering unambiguous.

## Why this approach
- Splitting CP/DP into separate releases is the only reliable way to guarantee ordering across helm upgrades.
- Makes rollbacks safer: rollback of DP can proceed without forcing CP rollback unless explicitly required.
- Enables independent readiness gating and different timeouts for CP vs DP.

## Testing
- `helm lint` on all charts
- `ct` chart-testing with kind
- Added an upgrade test scenario:
  - Install CP+DP at N-1
  - Upgrade CP to N
  - Verify CP ready/migrations complete
  - Upgrade DP to N
  - Inject a failure (CP deployment unavailable) and assert DP upgrade is blocked by readiness gate

## Notes for release engineering
- The installer should call:
  1) `helm upgrade --install redwood-private-cp charts/redwood-private-control-plane`
  2) `helm upgrade --install redwood-private-dp charts/redwood-private-data-plane`
- `charts/redwood-private` remains as the version pin + documentation entrypoint, but it is not intended to be installed as a single release for production Private.

## Follow-ups
- Add explicit "supported upgrade path" checks in installer plan generation to enforce CP then DP (tracked in ENG-4821)
- Expand gate checks for OIDC reachability and KMS (tracked in ENG-4829)

## Snippet (gate job)
The DP chart adds the following hook job:

apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: {{ include "redwood-private.fullname" . }}-cp-readiness-gate
  annotations:
    "helm.sh/hook": pre-install,pre-upgrade
    "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded
spec:
  activeDeadlineSeconds: {{ .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.timeoutSeconds }}
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
        - name: gate
          image: {{ .Values.global.toolsImage }}
          command: ["/bin/sh","-c"]
          args:
            - >
              ./wait-for-deployments.sh -n {{ .Release.Namespace }} {{ join " " .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.controlPlaneDeployments }}
              && ./http-check.sh http://{{ .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.controlPlaneService }}:{{ .Values.dataPlane.upgradeGate.controlPlanePort }}/readyz
              && ./migration-check.sh

(Full implementation lives in `charts/redwood-private-data-plane/templates/hooks/cp-readiness-gate.yaml` + `files/` scripts.)

charts: introduce redwood-private-data-plane chart scaffold
charts: convert redwood-private into umbrella chart with split values
dp: add cp readiness gate hook job + scripts
tests: add kind upgrade scenario for cp->dp sequencing
docs: add helm-direct migration steps + value mapping table
ci_checks: chart-testing / ct upgrade scenario (N-1 -> N) — PASS
conversation: Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): Splitting into two releases makes sense. Can we add explicit docs for helm-direct users (names of the two releases + ordering) and call out that the umbrella chart isn't meant to be installed? Also please ensure we keep a deprecation window for old values where possible.

Irene Choi (author): Added `charts/redwood-private/README.md` migration section with exact commands + a value mapping table. Kept aliases for `api.*` and `runtime.*` under the new `controlPlane.*`/`dataPlane.*` blocks for one release cycle; warnings are emitted via NOTES.txt.

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Gate timeout default 900s feels high but ok. Can we make sure the hook job doesn't stick around on failure (cleanup policy) and that failures are actionable (log which CP deployment or endpoint failed)?

Irene Choi (author): Updated hook-delete-policy to `before-hook-creation` only; on failure the Job remains for inspection (standard helm behavior). Added explicit log lines for each check (deployment availability, /readyz status code, migration status) and the failing condition exits with a unique code.

Benji Okafor (Platform): For migration check, relying on Job completion is brittle if customers disable the migrator and run manual migrations. Can the gate fall back to checking schema_version in DB if configured?

Irene Choi (author): Yep. `migration-check.sh` now supports two modes: (1) migrator job completion (default) and (2) DB schema_version check when `dataPlane.upgradeGate.dbCheck.enabled=true`. Documented in values + README. Default remains job-based to avoid DB creds in the DP release unless explicitly enabled.

Victor Alvarez (Networking): The gate hits CP service over cluster DNS. For some locked-down installs, NetworkPolicies may block the hook job pod. Can we ensure the job uses the same labels/serviceaccount as DP so customers' policies cover it?

Irene Choi (author): Hook job now uses `data-plane` serviceAccount + labels consistent with DP pods (`app.kubernetes.io/component=data-plane`). README includes a note: allow egress from DP namespace pods to CP service on port 8080 (or your configured port).
Rafael Mendes (On-call): Please add a short "what to do when gate blocks" section to NOTES so operators don't just rerun helm blindly.

Irene Choi (author): Added NOTES.txt output: shows failing check category and points to `kubectl logs job/<...>-cp-readiness-gate` plus the runbook section (link placeholder for now; will be finalized with docs PR).
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Approved after the added logging + clarified failure retention. This should reduce partial-upgrade incidents.
Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): Approved. We'll align installer plan stages to match CP then DP in the release train.
release_notes: Private Helm packaging now separates control-plane and data-plane into two charts/releases to guarantee upgrade ordering.
Data-plane installs/upgrades are gated on control-plane readiness + migration completion to prevent partial upgrades.
Umbrella `redwood-private` chart is now a documentation/version pin entrypoint; operators should install CP then DP as separate releases.

source.title: enhance-helm-templates-with-sequenced-init-pipelines-and-rollback-guardrails
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Introduce a tenant-scoped sequenced init pipeline and safe-rollback flags to the private installer Helm charts. Motivation: existing init hooks run concurrently per release which led to sporadic race conditions on tenant resources during multi-tenant upgrades and made rollbacks risky when migrations partially applied. This PR adds a lightweight sequencing layer in templates plus a small values schema extension to control pipeline stages, concurrency, and rollback behavior. Changes include: (1) new values keys under .Values.initPipeline to define ordered stages, per-stage timeouts, and 'waitFor: <k8sCondition>' expressions; (2) templating helpers (templates/_init-pipeline.yaml, templates/_pipeline-helpers.tpl) that render Job/JobTemplate groups with an annotation-based sequencer (release-annotations used as semi-transactional locks); (3) an optional preflight stage that runs a dry-run migration and checks CR readiness, gated by .Values.initPipeline.preflight.enabled; (4) a safeRollback boolean that, when true, switches post-upgrade hooks to non-destructive reconciliation-only jobs and preserves migration state in a release-scoped ConfigMap instead of tearing it down; (5) small values-schema additions to values.schema.json to validate new fields and document allowed stage names; (6) chart README additions and migration notes for operators. The design prioritizes backward compatibility: if .Values.initPipeline.enabled is unset or false we keep current hook behavior. Rollout plan: enable feature off by default, opt-in via values for Private installs, run canary installs in staging for two weeks, then flip default in a minor release after validation. Testing: unit template tests for the new helpers, integration smoke in CI (k3s) that simulates concurrent tenant upgrades, and a manual dry-run with on-prem QA. See commits and CI details below.
commits: a1f4b2c Add init pipeline helper templates and sequencer annotations
b3d9e8f Add values.schema.json entries for initPipeline and safeRollback
c7f2de1 Add pipeline preflight dry-run job and migration dry-run helper
d4a1b6a Update README and upgrade notes for private installer operators
e2c6f90 Add unit template tests and a k3s integration workflow
ci_checks: lint: yamllint & helm lint - initial run flagged long lines in README (fixed) - PASS
unit: chart template unit tests - PASS
integration: k3s init-pipeline smoke - initial run failed due to race in annotation lock; added short jitter - PASS
review_comments: Marco Li: Can you explain why we chose an annotation-based sequencer instead of leader-election via a controller? Concerned about robustness across kube api server restarts.

Priya Natarajan: Annotation locks are intentionally lightweight and avoid adding an operator process to the private installer. We added timeouts and a small jitter to minimize livelock. For larger installs we recommend using external orchestration; we also added guidance in README about switching to a controller if desired.
Elena Ortiz: Please validate values.schema.json changes with helm schema tests and add a clear migration note for operators who rely on pre-upgrade hooks.
Priya Natarajan: Added schema tests and expanded upgrade notes; included a sample values.yaml snippet for enabling safeRollback and a migration checklist.
Samir Patel: Will safeRollback leave behind ConfigMaps forever? We should include an explicit TTL policy or cleanup job.

Priya Natarajan: safeRollback preserves state for up to .Values.initPipeline.rollbackStateTtlDays (default 30). The cleanup job is included but disabled by default; operators can opt-in in values.yaml. Documented in README.
source.release_notes: Added optional sequenced tenant init pipeline with preflight dry-run, configurable per-stage timeouts, and safeRollback mode preserving migration state for safer rollbacks. Feature is opt-in; see chart README for migration steps and values examples.
notes_for_ops: Default behavior is unchanged; to opt-in set charts.private.initPipeline.enabled=true and configure stages in values.yaml.
safeRollback=true will change post-upgrade hooks to non-destructive reconcile jobs and store migration state in a ConfigMap for up to initPipeline.rollbackStateTtlDays days.
Operators should run the included k3s smoke test in staging and validate their migration scripts with preflight.enabled=true prior to enabling in production.
If you need stronger guarantees consider deploying the optional sequencer controller referenced in README (link in chart docs) instead of annotation-based locks.

source.title: Add Helm values for Runtime LTS channel selection + release notes URL annotations
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description:
Context: Dedicated + Private customers are asking to pin a stable Runtime LTS patch for 6–12+ months and to have a predictable way to discover “slower-change” release notes for the pinned version. This PR adds explicit Helm values to select the Runtime LTS channel/tag, surfaces the release notes URL via annotations, and documents expected pinning semantics for Private deployments.

What changed (high level): 1) New values to select the runtime image tag via an LTS-friendly interface (channel + version), while preserving existing explicit image.tag overrides. 2) Add standardized annotations on the runtime Deployment/StatefulSet (and optional Service) to expose the runtime version and release notes URL for operators/tools. 3) Docs: update chart README with examples and precedence rules (explicit tag override wins).

Values added: - runtime.channel: "stable" (default) | "lts" - runtime.lts.version: e.g. "1.18.1-lts" (required when channel=lts unless runtime.image.tag is set) - runtime.lts.releaseNotesUrl: optional explicit override; otherwise auto-constructed from runtime.lts.version - runtime.annotations.releaseNotes: true (default) to emit release notes annotation - runtime.annotations.version: true (default) to emit runtime version annotation

Annotation keys (new): - redwood.ai/runtime-version: "1.18.1-lts" - redwood.ai/runtime-release-notes: "https://docs.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts" These are intended to be machine-readable for fleet tooling and to match what Product is planning to surface in Console during LTS pinning.

Precedence rules (documented): 1) If runtime.image.tag is set, we use it as-is and set redwood.ai/runtime-version to that value (no parsing). 2) Else if runtime.channel=lts, require runtime.lts.version and use that as runtime.image.tag. 3) Else default behavior remains unchanged (stable channel uses Chart appVersion / values default).

Why annotations instead of ConfigMap only: Operators frequently inspect the rendered workload objects directly, and SRE tooling can scrape annotations without following references. This also gives Private customers an auditable “what version is running” signal that matches pinned artifacts.

Testing: - chart-testing (ct lint + install) - helm template snapshot checks for both stable and lts paths - verified annotations render on Deployment and are stable across upgrades

Rollout notes: This is backwards compatible for existing installs unless they were relying on an unset image.tag but a non-standard appVersion behavior. No runtime behavior changes; this is packaging-only.

Follow-ups: - Once Runtime LTS release notes generator output URL scheme is finalized, we may adjust the default releaseNotesUrl constructor (keeping explicit override). - Consider emitting the release notes annotation on Pods (via podTemplate annotations) as well; currently it’s on the owning workload and podTemplate to cover both kubectl get deploy and kubectl get pods use cases.
commits: chart(runtime): add runtime.channel + runtime.lts.version values and tag resolution helper
chart(runtime): emit runtime version and release notes annotations (workload + pod template)
docs: document LTS pinning semantics and examples in runtime chart README
tests: add helm template golden outputs for lts/stable tag precedence
notable_snippets:
values.yaml: runtime:
  channel: stable
  lts:
    version: ""
    releaseNotesUrl: ""
  annotations:

title: Add Helm values for Runtime LTS channel selection + release notes URL annotations
preview:
Context: Dedicated + Private customers are asking to pin a stable Runtime LTS patch for 6–12+ months and to have a predictable way to discover “slower-change” release notes for the pinned version. This PR adds explicit Helm values to select the Runtime LTS channel/tag, surfaces the release notes URL via annotations, and documents expected pinning semantics for Private deployments.

What changed (high level): 1) New values to select the runtime image tag via an LTS-friendly interface (channel + version), while preserving existing explicit image.tag overrides. 2) Add standardized annotations on the runtime Deployment/StatefulSet (and optional Service) to expose the runtime version and release notes URL for operators/tools. 3) Docs: update chart README with examples and precedence rules (explicit tag override wins).

Values added: - runtime.channel: "stable" (default) | "lts" - runtime.lts.version: e.g. "1.18.1-lts" (required when channel=lts unless runtime.image.tag is set) - runtime.lts.releaseNotesUrl: optional explicit override; otherwise auto-constructed from runtime.lts.version - runtime.annotations.releaseNotes: true (default) to emit release notes annotation - runtime.annotations.version: true (default) to emit runtime version annotation

Annotation keys (new): - redwood.ai/runtime-version: "1.18.1-lts" - redwood.ai/runtime-release-notes: "https://docs.redwood.ai/runtime/lts/1.18.1-lts" These are intended to be machine-readable for fleet tooling and to match what Product is planning to surface in Console during LTS pinning.

Precedence rules (documented): 1) If runtime.image.tag is set, we use it as-is and set redwood.ai/runtime-version to that value (no parsing). 2) Else if runtime.channel=lts, require runtime.lts.version and use that as runtime.image.tag. 3) Else default behavior remains unchanged (stable channel uses Chart appVersion / values default).

Why annotations instead of ConfigMap only: Operators frequently inspect the rendered workload objects directly, and SRE tooling can scrape annotations without following references. This also gives Private customers an auditable “what version is running” signal that matches pinned artifacts.

Testing: - chart-testing (ct lint + install) - helm template snapshot checks for both stable and lts paths - verified annotations render on Deployment and are stable across upgrades

Rollout notes: This is backwards compatible for existing installs unless they were relying on an unset image.tag but a non-standard appVersion behavior. No runtime behavior changes; this is packaging-only.

Follow-ups: - Once Runtime LTS release notes generator output URL scheme is finalized, we may adjust the default releaseNotesUrl constructor (keeping explicit override). - Consider emitting the release notes annotation on Pods (via podTemplate annotations) as well; currently it’s on the owning workload and podTemplate to cover both kubectl get deploy and kubectl get pods use cases.

source.title: Add restore Job template + Helm hooks for safe restore-time validations
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.release_notes: Private Helm charts: add optional restore Job + Helm hook flow to run post-restore validations (smoke checks) with conservative defaults; no behavior change unless explicitly enabled via values.
source.description: Context: As part of Private Day-2 ops (backup/restore + DR), we need a deterministic way to run restore-time validations inside the cluster without relying on ad-hoc kubectl commands or long-lived pods. This PR adds a templated Kubernetes Job to the `redwood-private` chart and wires Helm hook annotations so validations run at the right time during `helm upgrade/install` restore workflows. The goal is to make restores safer (catch missing CRDs / wrong ordering / unhealthy control-plane) while avoiding destructive behavior (no writes, no migrations, no secret material). What’s included: (1) New `templates/restore/restore-validation-job.yaml` Job template that executes `redwood-private-installer restore validate` (or a configurable command) against in-cluster control-plane endpoints. (2) Hook annotations: `post-install,post-upgrade` with `hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded` so we don’t leave Jobs lying around and we can re-run reliably. (3) RBAC: a narrow Role/RoleBinding scoped to reading the minimum required resources (pods/services/endpoints/configmaps in the release namespace). No cluster-wide list permissions. (4) Values and guardrails: feature is OFF by default; when enabled, the job uses a dedicated serviceAccount, a hard timeout, conservative resources, and an explicit allowlist for which checks may run. (5) Output: the job writes a short JSON report to stdout (and optionally to a ConfigMap) so SRE/Support can attach it to tickets. Non-goals / out of scope: This PR does not implement the actual restore itself (handled by installer CLI), does not snapshot etcd, and does not include secret values in any rendered manifests. Usage: `helm upgrade --install redwood-private ./charts/redwood-private -f values.yaml --set restore.validation.enabled=true --set restore.validation.mode=post-restore` (see values docs in this PR). Notes: Hook ordering is tricky when customers run a restore into a fresh cluster where CRDs are installed in the same operation. This PR ensures the validation Job is gated behind `.Values.crds.install` and will short-circuit with a clear error message if expected APIs are missing. Related: ENG-9822 (restore apply workflow), ENG-9827 (restore drill automation), ENG-9828 (runbooks).
commits: helm: add restore validation job template and values scaffold
helm: add hook annotations + delete policy; prevent job from running when disabled
rbac: add minimal role for validation job; use dedicated serviceAccount
docs: update values.yaml comments + README restore section
fix: tolerate missing CRDs; emit actionable failure reason
illustrative_snippets:
values.yaml: restore:
  validation:
    enabled: false
    hook:
      enabled: true
      events: "post-install,post-upgrade"
      deletePolicy: "before-hook-creation,hook-succeeded"
    image:
      repository: "ghcr.io/redwoodinference/private-installer"
      tag: "{{ .Chart.AppVersion }}"
    command: ["/installer", "restore", "validate"]
    timeoutSeconds: 900
    report:
      writeConfigMap: true
      configMapName: "{{ include \"redwood-private.fullname\" . }}-restore-validation"
    rbac:
      create: true
      serviceAccountName: "{{ include \"redwood-private.fullname\" . }}-restore-validation"

restore-validation-job.yaml: metadata:
  annotations:
    "helm.sh/hook": "{{ .Values.restore.validation.hook.events }}"
    "helm.sh/hook-weight": "10"
    "helm.sh/hook-delete-policy": "{{ .Values.restore.validation.hook.deletePolicy }}"
spec:
  backoffLimit: 0
  activeDeadlineSeconds: {{ .Values.restore.validation.timeoutSeconds }}
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      serviceAccountName: {{ .Values.restore.validation.rbac.serviceAccountName | quote }}
      containers:
      - name: restore-validate
        image: "{{ .Values.restore.validation.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.restore.validation.image.tag }}"
        command: {{ toJson .Values.restore.validation.command }}
        env:
        - name: REDWOOD_NAMESPACE
          value: {{ .Release.Namespace | quote }}
conversation: Sean Gallagher (review): Hook behavior looks right, but we should ensure we don’t page or block upgrades in non-prod if the validation check is flaky. Can we make failure behavior configurable (fail-open vs fail-closed)?

Bruno Silva (author): Added `restore.validation.failPolicy` with `Fail` default for prod guidance, and documented `Warn` for non-prod/drills. In `Warn` mode the job exits 0 but prints a clear `VALIDATION_WARN` summary and still writes the report ConfigMap.

Hanae Suzuki (review): Please confirm we’re not rendering any secrets into the Job env. Also, the report ConfigMap might include sensitive endpoint URLs; can we gate it behind a value and default to stdout-only?

Bruno Silva (author): Confirmed: no secret data is templated; token/credentials are not referenced. Report ConfigMap now defaults to `writeConfigMap=false`; enabling it requires explicit opt-in. Also redacted hostnames in the JSON report by default (keeps service names + namespace).

Ethan Park (review): Concern: if CRDs are missing (fresh cluster) we’ll fail the hook and make `helm install` confusing. Can we detect missing API resources and emit a very explicit message pointing to the CRD install step?

Bruno Silva (author): Added a preflight that checks for `redwood.ai/*` CRDs via discovery (kubectl-equivalent) and exits with `ERR_MISSING_CRDS` plus next steps. Also added a note in NOTES.txt that restore validation hooks should run after CRDs are applied (or use the separate CRD chart).

Fatima Noor (review): For air-gapped customers, image repository override needs to be straightforward. Can we ensure the restore validation job uses the same global image registry override as the rest of the chart?
Bruno Silva (author): Updated templates to respect `.Values.global.imageRegistry` and `.Values.global.imagePullSecrets`. Added README example for mirrored registries.
Sean Gallagher (review): Please set `backoffLimit: 0` so we don’t burn time retrying a deterministic failure, and cap runtime with `activeDeadlineSeconds`.
Bruno Silva (author): Done (backoffLimit=0, activeDeadlineSeconds wired to values with default 900s).
Ethan Park (approval): Looks good. The hook-weight and delete policy match our existing migration job patterns. Approving after CI green.
Sean Gallagher (approval): Approved. Please make sure runbooks mention the `Warn` failPolicy for staging restore drills.
checks: security: kube-score (best-effort): pass (1 warning: readOnlyRootFilesystem recommended)
post_merge_followups: Update DR runbook to reference `restore.validation.*` values and recommended settings for staging drills (ENG-9828).
Confirm installer image tag alignment with chart AppVersion for Private releases (ENG-9822).
Add alerting guidance: do not page on hook failures in non-prod; rely on restore drill job metrics (ENG-9827).

source.title: private: add optional scheduled backup CronJob + service account/RBAC + resource defaults
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description:
This PR adds first-class Helm support for scheduled backups in Redwood Private by introducing an optional Kubernetes CronJob and associated service account/RBAC wiring. This is the chart-side counterpart to the installer `backup create` command (ENG-9821) and the Day-2 backup/restore toolkit workstream. The CronJob is disabled by default and can be enabled via values. It is designed to be safe-by-default (concurrency policy, history limits, backoff limits), to run in restricted clusters (no cluster-wide RBAC required), and to support both VPC and on-prem installs where outbound egress may be limited.

What’s included: (1) New values under `backup.cronJob.*` to control scheduling and runtime behavior, including schedule, suspend, startingDeadlineSeconds, concurrencyPolicy, ttlSecondsAfterFinished, and history limits. (2) `backup.serviceAccount.*` and `backup.rbac.*` knobs to create or reuse a dedicated service account with minimal namespace-scoped permissions. (3) Resource requests/limits and pod-level controls (nodeSelector/tolerations/affinity, priorityClassName, securityContext, podSecurityContext) so SRE/customers can place the job on appropriate nodes and avoid starving the control plane. (4) A thin wrapper command that invokes the private installer backup flow with consistent flags, logs, and exit codes; backup artifacts are still written to the configured backend (e.g., S3/NFS) as defined elsewhere.

Security posture: the CronJob does not require cluster-admin. By default it does not request permissions to read Kubernetes Secrets; credentials are expected to be provided via mounted secret/environment (standard K8s volume mounts) rather than via API reads. The Role is namespace-scoped and only grants read access to selected configmaps used for identifying release metadata and environment configuration. The container is configured to run as non-root, drop Linux capabilities, and disallow privilege escalation.

Operational notes: Default schedule is unset (must be explicitly configured), and `backup.cronJob.enabled=false` by default. The job retries are bounded. Logs are structured and include a short summary line that can be scraped by log-based alerts until the metrics work lands (ENG-9826). This PR also documents common failure modes we’ve seen in staging (INT-612: missing RBAC permissions / running under the wrong service account) and provides a recommended minimal configuration.

Example values:
- backup:
  cronJob:
    enabled: true
    schedule: "0 */6 * * *"
    concurrencyPolicy: "Forbid"
    successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
    failedJobsHistoryLimit: 3
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "256Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
  serviceAccount:
    create: true
    name: "redwood-private-backup"
  rbac:
    create: true

Rollout/compatibility: This chart change is backward compatible. If enabled, it assumes the `redwood-private-installer` image tag referenced by the chart includes the `backup create` command; we fail fast with a clear error message if the binary is missing. Release notes included in chart README.
commits: charts/redwood-private: add backup CronJob template + values wiring
charts/redwood-private: add ServiceAccount/Role/RoleBinding for backup job (namespace-scoped)
charts/redwood-private: document backup.cronJob configuration + example values
charts/redwood-private: set default securityContext, resource requests/limits, and job controls
notable_snippets: CronJob template (excerpt): apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: CronJob metadata: name: {{ include "redwood-private.fullname" . }}-backup spec: schedule: {{ required "backup.cronJob.schedule is required when enabled" .Values.backup.cronJob.schedule | quote }} concurrencyPolicy: {{ .Values.backup.cronJob.concurrencyPolicy | default "Forbid" | quote }} jobTemplate: spec: backoffLimit: {{ .Values.backup.cronJob.backoffLimit | default 1 }} template: spec: serviceAccountName: {{ include "redwood-private.backupServiceAccountName" . }} securityContext: {{- toYaml .Values.backup.cronJob.podSecurityContext | nindent 12 }} containers: - name: backup image: {{ include "redwood-private.installerImage" . }} args: ["backup","create","--output","{{ .Values.backup.outputPath }}"] resources: {{- toYaml .Values.backup.cronJob.resources | nindent 16 }}

RBAC (excerpt): kind: Role rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["configmaps"] verbs: ["get","list"] resourceNames: ["redwood-private-release", "redwood-private-env"] (no Secrets API permissions by default)
source.review_conversation:
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Please make CronJob disabled by default and require schedule when enabled; also set concurrencyPolicy=Forbid and keep history limits low so clusters don’t accumulate job objects. Bruno Silva: Done. Added `required` on schedule when enabled, defaulted concurrencyPolicy to Forbid, and set successful/failed history defaults to 3.

Hanae Suzuki (Security): Can we avoid granting read permissions on Secrets? We’ve been trying to enforce “mount secrets, don’t read them via API” as a pattern for restricted clusters. Also please ensure container runs non-root and drops capabilities to satisfy PSA-restricted baselines. Bruno Silva: Updated Role to only read specific configmaps (no secrets) and added securityContext defaults: runAsNonRoot=true, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false, readOnlyRootFilesystem=true, capabilities.drop=["ALL"].

Connor O’Brien (Release): Please call out version compatibility: enabling this requires the installer image tag that contains `backup create`. Also please keep args/flags stable (we’ll reference this from internal runbooks). Bruno Silva: Added a README section and a fail-fast check in the wrapper command. Also pinned the default arg shape and documented overrides via `backup.cronJob.extraArgs`.

Ethan Park (Control Plane): The job name should be deterministic and include release name; also ensure it runs in the same namespace as the control plane so it can hit in-cluster endpoints without extra network rules. Bruno Silva: Updated naming to `{{ fullname }}-backup` and confirmed namespace-scoped templates; added note in README about network policies and in-cluster DNS target.

Follow-up requested changes: rename `backup.enabled` to `backup.cronJob.enabled` to avoid confusion with “backup feature”. Completed in latest commit; schema updated accordingly.

Approvals: Sean Gallagher approved after RBAC/securityContext changes; Hanae Suzuki approved after removing Secrets API permissions; Connor O’Brien approved after doc/compatibility notes.
source.release_notes: Helm: add optional scheduled backup CronJob for Redwood Private with dedicated service account/RBAC and configurable resources. Disabled by default; requires setting backup.cronJob.schedule when enabled. No cluster-wide permissions required.

      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
  serviceAccount:
    create: true
    name: "redwood-private-backup"
  rbac:
    create: true

Rollout/compatibility: This chart change is backward compatible. If enabled, it assumes the `redwood-private-installer` image tag referenced by the chart includes the `backup create` command; we fail fast with a clear error message if the binary is missing. Release notes included in chart README.

source.title: Vendorize Helm values and add init-backoff controller for private deploys
source.author: Maya Thompson
source.release_notes:
- Vendorize and lock chart values for private installer to support air-gapped packaging.
- Add init-backoff-controller: idempotent init job backoff and TTL management for tenant seeding.
- Add tooling script to vend values bundles and generate deterministic checksums for offline installs.
source.description:
Motivation:
Private (VPC/on-prem) customers packaging charts for air-gapped installs need a reproducible, audited set of values that we ship and lock during a release. In addition, tenant bootstrap/init jobs in some clusters were observed to fail and retry indefinitely when external services (DB, image registry) were slow to respond. This causes init race conditions and makes rolling upgrades fragile.

What this PR does (single continuous description):
- Vendorizes chart values: a new charts/private/vendored-values/ directory contains per-version, normalized values.yaml fragments and a values.bundle.json with checksums. We ship a small helper script (ci/airgap/vend-values.sh) that creators can run to produce an airgap bundle. The vendored values are used by the installer pipeline when building on-prem bundles so customers get a deterministic, reviewed values set instead of relying on live defaults.
- Adds an init-backoff-controller: a lightweight controller template (templates/init-backoff-controller.yaml) plus a controller configmap and RBAC that wraps tenant init Jobs with a controlled exponential backoff and TTL controller. The controller ensures: idempotent requeueing, jittered backoff to avoid thundering herds, and a fixed TTL after which a failing init job is marked for manual retry.
- Templating improvements: introduce a new helper tpl 'vendorValuesLookup' and a values.schema.json change to validate bundle reference fields. The helpers allow charts to reference vendored fragments by name (eg, vendor://v1.9/connector-overrides) so the installer can substitute offline content at package time.
- Backwards compatibility: If vendored-values is absent, charts fall back to existing values paths. The init controller is guarded by values.private.initBackoff.enabled (false by default) so clusters are opt-in.

Checklist:
- [x] Add vendored-values directory and generation script
- [x] Add templates and RBAC for init-backoff-controller
- [x] Add values.schema.json entries and unit tests for schema validation
- [x] CI: helm lint + template + schema validation
- [x] Integration: run upgrade canary in kind cluster, simulate slow DB and airgapped asset staging
- [x] Docs: update private-installer README with packaging steps and controller notes

Testing and validation performed:
- Helm lint against charts/private and charts/common (all checks pass).
- Unit schema validation with 'charts-validate' harness (new cases added for vendored bundle refs).
- Integration in a local KIND cluster: exercised tenant bootstrap with DB delayed by 30-120s; init job restarted using backoff controller and eventually succeeded once DB came online. Verified TTL semantics stop infinite retries after configured threshold.
- Packaged an airgap bundle with ci/airgap/vend-values.sh and verified the installer pipeline consumes the bundle and renders identical manifests in an offline environment.

Operational notes (rollout):
- Feature flag path: values.private.initBackoff.enabled (default: false). Recommend enabling on a small canary fleet first.
- Airgap bundles should be created and checked into release assets; installer CI will fail if the referenced bundle checksum is missing to avoid silent drift.

Security and compliance: the vendored-values bundle is treated as a release artifact and added to the release signing step. The init-backoff-controller RBAC is scoped to tenant namespaces only; no cluster-admin permissions are introduced.

Related items: Linear ENG-4821 (airgap packaging improvements) and ENG-4893 (init job lifecycle hardening).

Merging notes: squash merge to keep vendoring commits atomic and make rollbacks easier.

review_comments: Diego Alvarez (2024-11-08): Can we ensure the vendored-values checksum is validated at install-time? Suggest adding a preflight check in the installer pipeline.
Author reply (Maya Thompson, 2024-11-08): Added a checksum validation step in ci/airgap/vend-values.sh and the installer's pack step now fails fast if checksum missing.

Priya Natarajan (2024-11-09): The RBAC looks fine but please limit the controller's Namespace watch to tenant namespaces only. Also prefer a ConfigMap to tune backoff params rather than values so ops can patch runtime behavior.

Author reply (2024-11-10): Controller now uses a namespaced Role/RoleBinding and consumes a ConfigMap (templates/init-backoff-configmap.yaml) to allow runtime tuning. Values still control enablement and default params.
Evan Grant (2024-11-11): Verified integration test runs; one flake observed in kind when image pull policy was IfNotPresent. Recommend explicit imagePullPolicy: Always in test harness.
tests_run: integration: kind cluster tenant bootstrap with delayed DB
source.risk_assessment: Low-medium: vendoring is additive and guarded by fallback behavior. Init-backoff-controller is opt-in. Risk is operational complexity if operators enable without reading docs; mitigated by clear release notes and default-off flag.
